width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7bf0da0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffa%2Fba%2Fd8659c534d158251eda36a462c59%2Fpartner-photo-credit-galdones-photography.jpg" alt="Partner Photo_credit Galdones Photography.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7bf0da0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffa%2Fba%2Fd8659c534d158251eda36a462c59%2Fpartner-photo-credit-galdones-photography.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d0715c9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffa%2Fba%2Fd8659c534d158251eda36a462c59%2Fpartner-photo-credit-galdones-photography.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7bf0da0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffa%2Fba%2Fd8659c534d158251eda36a462c59%2Fpartner-photo-credit-galdones-photography.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Zubair Mohajir, Rishi Kumar and David Mor</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Galdones Photography</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b></b> “We went through years of working together where we were constantly talking about ‘How do we pay rent this month,’ or ‘Can we ask for some of our co-workers to accept their paychecks a little bit later than what their payday is.’ It's the worst feeling as a business owner to do. You're taught that if you can't do all these things perfectly, you shouldn't go into business.” <b></b></p><p>Mor points to the first year in business being the most difficult and the team has since found a pace that allows them to take big bets on not only the concepts, but their real estate locations, such as Mirra in Bucktown.</p><p>“The space Mirra’s in has turned over so many times, we were horrified by the fact that, ‘what if we're just another statistic?’” said Mor. “We have some element of delusion or audacity built into what we do that kind of allows us to say, ‘It can't be worse than what we're doing right now, which is not owning our own [restaurants].’ The landlord and neighbors told us that they've seen so many businesses go in there with very little success. Mirra was the biggest success story we could have ever imagined. We've taken that momentum and that energy and said there’s nothing telling me that can’t happen again in a new location.”</p><p>The seven hospitality concepts Mohajir owns with several different partners do not operate under a parent company. That might change one day but for now, Mohajir says he’s just having fun going into business with friends.</p><p>“I really love opening restaurants,” said Mohajir. “Most people think too long about what moves they want to make. I dream about it, but I really pride myself and my team’s ability to just go out and do it. The best calls I like to make are to friends saying, ‘Hey, quit your job, move back. Let's open a restaurant.’ You attack the world differently when you're walking in step with somebody.”</p><h3>Lamb Biryani</h3><p><b>By Chef Zubair Mohajir</b><br>Man, a succulent lamb biryani just hits the spot after a day of fasting. All is well after a few bites of this dish.</p><p><b>Ingredients</b></p><p><b>For the marinade</b></p><p>2 pounds lamb leg, cubed into 1-inch chunks<br>2 to 3 teaspoons biryani masala<br>1 tablespoon ginger paste<br>1 tablespoon garlic paste<br>2 teaspoons kosher salt<br>1 tablespoon kashmiri red chili powder<br>½ teaspoon ground turmeric<br>¼ cup fresh mint leaves, chopped<br>2 tablespoons lemon juice<br>¾ cup plain yogurt</p><p><b>For the crispy fried onions</b></p><p>4 tablespoons ghee<br>1 large yellow onion, thinly sliced</p><p><b>For the rice</b></p><p>2½ cups extra long grain basmati rice<br>1 teaspoon black cumin seeds (shah jeera)<br>2 bay leaves (tamal patra)<br>1 star anise<br>4 green cardamom pods<br>8 cloves<br>1½ tablespoons kosher salt</p><p><b>For the garnish</b></p><p>½ teaspoon saffron<br>2 tablespoons warm milk<br>¼ cup chopped cilantro</p><p><b>Directions</b></p><p>Add all of the marinade ingredients to a plastic zip-top bag and mix well. Marinate overnight in the refrigerator.</p><p>When you’re ready to cook the dish, in a saute pan over high heat, fry the thinly sliced onions until golden brown, about 10 minutes. </p><p>Combine all of the rice ingredients in 3 quarts of water in a large pot and let soak for one hour.</p><p>Mix the saffron and milk together and set aside.</p><p>In a deep pot, sear all the meat and cook 75 percent until just tender. Reduce until all the liquid is cooked out.</p><p>Strain the soaked rice and then add to the pot. Mix well and add just enough water to cover the meat and rice. </p><p>Add the sauteed onions. Mix in the milk and saffron mixture and cover.</p><p>Cook for 45 minutes and let it rest for 30 with the heat off. Garnish with cilantro and serve.</p>
entries.author: Ximena N. Beltran Quan Kiu

entries.title: Cases at Warlord, Noma prompt fresh questions about how to protect restaurant workers
entries.summary: <p>Already this year, a pair of tectonic restaurant industry news reports have exposed years of alleged staff abuses by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/01/26/trevor-fleming-warlord-restaurants-crime" target="_blank" ><u>Trevor Fleming</u></a>, the chef and former co-owner of Warlord, and <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/07/dining/rene-redzepi-noma-abuse-allegations.html" target="_blank" ><u>Rene Redzepi</u></a>, founding chef of Michelin-starred Copenhagen restaurant Noma.</p><p>Some observers expressed exasperation that the accusations went unchecked for so long despite, in each case, allegations that go back years. But restaurants remain hierarchical small businesses with few formal systems for employee complaints and power asymmetries between workers and the higher-ups, whose names often are on the door.</p><p>Still, in Chicago, at least two groups are trying to give hospitality workers a voice and forging a path toward levying formal complaints. The grassroots organizations <a class="Link" href="https://www.survivorsknow.org/" target="_blank" ><u>Survivors Know</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.thechaadproject.org/" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Hospitality Accountability & Advocacy Database</u></a> (the CHAAD Project) say they are doing what they can to offer advocacy and mental health support services, albeit with limited manpower and budgets.</p><p>But for survivors, the paths toward safety, healing, and, murkiest of all, accountability are fraught, slow-moving and routinely unsatisfying.</p><p>“The fear of retaliation is 100 percent real,” said a longtime hospitality worker and former employee of Warlord, where star chef Fleming was accused of<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/01/29/warlord-eatery-owner-accused-of-sharing-sexual-images-of-co-worker-released-from-monitoring-for-japan-trip" target="_blank" ><u> sharing nonconsensual sexual images of a worker</u></a>. “Sometimes there is no justice. It’s just removing yourself from the harm.”</p><p>Most restaurants operate on razor-thin profit margins, meaning stress-inducing urgency is built into their very fabric. The power imbalance is exacerbated by a tipped wage structure and the often intentionally blurry lines of conduct in an entertainment-adjacent industry. (Chicago is in the process of <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/small-business/2024/07/01/chicago-tipped-workers-five-year-plan-raise-subminimum-wages-restaurants" target="_blank" ><u>phasing out</u></a> tipped wages.)</p><p>Social justice advocates say part of the problem is chalking up workplace harassment to one bad seed as opposed to treating it like a community problem.</p><div 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/85074b8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7400x4153+0+391/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F77%2F76%2F77f787d04098959978f48e27609b%2Fwarlord-260127-001.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9d98838/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7400x4153+0+391/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F77%2F76%2F77f787d04098959978f48e27609b%2Fwarlord-260127-001.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A chef at the popular Avondale restaurant Warlord was charged earlier this year with sharing explicit photos of a coworker. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“What we’ve seen in Chicago and everywhere with the restaurant industry is there are these moments of ‘reckoning,’” said Jo Chernow, executive director of Survivors Know, a nonprofit advocating for survivors of power-based sexual violence. “If an individual chef is super toxic, there’s a wave of yeses then an acknowledgement that this is just part and parcel of the industry. Hyperfixation on individual chefs takes away from what this is, which is systemic and super normalized.”</p><p>To foster more interpersonal trust, a small but growing number of restaurants are engaging workers in internal policy and culture conversations. The movement has been galvanized by recent aggressive federal immigration tactics, which prompted honest conversations over how to respond and keep each other safe.</p><h3><b>Call HR? There’s no HR.</b></h3><h3></h3><p>According to police reports and multiple sources, Fleming allegedly shared sexual photos of women without their consent and created a toxic work environment by routinely berating Warlord employees. Fleming’s attorney didn’t respond to a request for comment by publication time.</p><p>The former Warlord employee said these alleged abuses were widely known at the Avondale restaurant, including by co-owners Emily Kraszyk and John Lupton. But the situation came to a head in January, when Fleming was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/01/26/trevor-fleming-warlord-restaurants-crime" target="_blank" ><u>charged</u></a> with sharing explicit images of a woman without her consent.</p><p>Worker abuse in Chicago’s hospitality industry isn’t exactly a secret, but answerability remains elusive. Being part of a high-turnover industry built on relationships, workers have little incentive to speak up if something untoward happens. Amid a shift away from workers’ rights initiated by the <a class="Link" href="https://www.epi.org/blog/trumps-department-of-labor-is-dismantling-key-workplace-protections/" target="_blank" ><u>Trump administration</u></a>, the onus falls on those in charge to do the right thing and victims to make a case — often multiple times at their own risk — for their pain.</p><p>The former Warlord employee mentioned the private Chicago Service Industry Facebook page, which has some 51,400 members, as one of few reliable places for calling out abusive employers.</p><p>Five years ago, a group of current and former hospitality workers formed the CHAAD project in hopes of helping workers document abuse and demand accountability.</p><p>Today, the group receives reports of workplace abuse and wage theft through its website, social media, the digital platform <a class="Link" href="https://shiftchange.us/" target="_blank" ><u>ShiftChange</u></a> (which CHAAD developed with Survivors Know and <a class="Link" href="https://www.workitlabs.org/" target="_blank" ><u>WorkIt Labs</u></a>) and less formally as individuals.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/27e6f29/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3e%2Ff0%2Fe54de235464cb189c01b752bf63a%2Frestoharassment-260416-9.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7e49a93/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3e%2Ff0%2Fe54de235464cb189c01b752bf63a%2Frestoharassment-260416-9.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“We don’t have a lot of policies that protect workers,” said Draper, a bartender at Consignment Lounge who helped organize the Chicago Hospitality Accountability & Advocacy Database (CHAAD) Project.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“We don’t have a lot of policies that protect workers,” said Raeghn Draper, executive director of the CHAAD project. Draper added that Illinois is also an at-will employment state, meaning employers can terminate employees at any time for any reason that isn’t illegal. “If your boss gets a whiff of you filing a complaint with an enforcement agency, you can get fired for that, and there’s not much to do in an at-will state unless you’re really rigorous.”</p><p>Abuse reports spike between April and November, when there are more jobs available so people feel less vulnerable about taking action.</p><p>“December through March is quieter, when jobs are harder to find and people want to hold onto the one they have, even if it’s sh—-,” said Draper, who also bartends at Consignment Lounge in Logan Square.</p><p>Workers can file reports in English or Spanish through ShiftChange. (Since securing grant funding for Survivors Know’s hospitality arm, ShiftChange is hiring a new, native-Spanish-speaking lead organizer.) An advisor will follow up with an interview about next steps. If a worker is too afraid to leave their job or report abuse internally, advisors will help them identify a safety plan to recognize what they can lean on, externally and within. How do they get to work? Can they call someone to pick them up midday?</p><p>“By starting to map their own safety, people can start identifying their own power,” Chernow said. “We do the same thing with survivors planning to leave their abusers. So many of those workplace dynamics mirror domestic violence dynamics.”</p><h3><b>Navigating uncomfortable terrain</b></h3><h3></h3><p>One of Fleming’s victims told WBEZ/Sun-Times that when she learned that he was reportedly sharing sexually explicit photos of her without her consent, she feared for her safety most as she weighed her options.</p><p>Friends and family repeatedly discouraged her from contacting the police, citing concerns about her welfare, she said, asking to remain anonymous for security reasons. She contacted a lawyer instead, who said the firm could only assist with civil cases and couldn’t advise on criminal matters, “which added confusion and frustration about how to move forward,” she said.</p><p>She found a path ahead in part after meeting with a support group through the Chicago trauma and abuse support organization <a class="Link" href="https://www.womenrisechicago.org/" target="_blank" ><u>Women Rise Chicago</u></a>, which also offers free one-to-one counseling. She decided to file a report with the police.</p><p>When survivor communities overlap, Survivors Know will connect victims to solidarity circles, which can make people feel less alone and help them draw contextual connections between singular experiences and systemic failures, Chernow said. The organization supports those seeking recourse by sending letters to restaurants asking them to meaningfully address worker allegations and drafting press advisories.</p><div class="Enhancement" night after the James Beard Awards at the Lyric Opera House in Chicago on June 16, 2025." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/44df202/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F3a%2F9f1aa13b4377869b8284ba3606ec%2Fbeardliveparty-0616250188.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f37a5ba/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F3a%2F9f1aa13b4377869b8284ba3606ec%2Fbeardliveparty-0616250188.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/44df202/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F3a%2F9f1aa13b4377869b8284ba3606ec%2Fbeardliveparty-0616250188.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A chef serves up post-ceremony food at a reception at Union Station during the James Beard Awards. A conversation has been unfolding in recent years about how to protect workers from abuse in high-pressure kitchens. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In the case of Warlord, Survivors Know submitted a letter requesting a third-party investigation of the allegations and recommending mandatory code of conduct and comprehensive anti-harassment training. According to Survivors Know, attorneys for the restaurant claimed their firm’s investigation found no wrongdoing, but didn’t respond when the group requested the findings.</p><p>Kraszyk and Lupton have since <a class="Link" href="https://blockclubchicago.org/2026/01/29/warlord-co-owners-call-allegations-against-chef-and-partner-trevor-fleming-reprehensible/" target="_blank" ><u>broken ranks with Fleming</u></a> and are <a class="Link" href="https://blockclubchicago.org/2026/03/10/warlord-on-brink-of-ruin-after-business-plummets-due-to-former-chefs-abusive-behavior-lawsuit/" target="_blank" ><u>suing</u></a> their former partner.</p><p>The broader goal of organizations that help hospitality workers, Chernow said, is to tangibly improve working conditions, “not to shut down restaurants.” But they’re also balancing victims’ comfort levels as they navigate painful terrain.</p><p>Draper, too, said initial action can fizzle when survivors see what’s required of them to pursue recourse.</p><p>Indeed, as the woman who filed the police report against Fleming later told me, “You do it scared; that’s the reality. You might not know what to do, and you’re doing each step scared.”</p><h3><b>A better future? </b></h3><h3></h3><p>For a restaurant industry battered by inflation, tariffs and rising costs for health care and labor, it’s likely not the moment to implement systemic overhauls to workplace culture.</p><p>CHAAD has spent the past few years studying cooperative and shared ownership restaurant models, and is piloting a Chicago worker-owned restaurant called <a class="Link" href="https://www.thechaadproject.org/radish-eats" target="_blank" ><u>Rad-Ish Eats</u></a>, which now operates as a popular seasonal pop-up series.</p><p>“Research shows democratic ownership and shared leadership are far more sustainable in terms of shared risk and ownership responsibility,” Draper said. “It’s a shared workplace; the money made is shared, everybody should be involved.”</p><p>Former Noma employees felt compelled to speak out when Redzepi announced his $1,500-per-night series of Los Angeles pop-up dinners, which reminded them that “his empire was built on their work, and their pain,” wrote Julia Moskin, the New York Times reporter who broke the Noma story. Redzepi has since <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/11/dining/rene-redzepi-noma-resign-abuse.html" target="_blank" ><u>stepped down</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/07c6bf2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6332x3554+0+335/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff8%2F58%2Ff2c888c64cbf86cbfffc2a39a332%2Fbeardlive-0616251435-enhanced-nr.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c280c1f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6332x3554+0+335/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff8%2F58%2Ff2c888c64cbf86cbfffc2a39a332%2Fbeardlive-0616251435-enhanced-nr.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/07c6bf2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6332x3554+0+335/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff8%2F58%2Ff2c888c64cbf86cbfffc2a39a332%2Fbeardlive-0616251435-enhanced-nr.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Julia Momosé of Kumiko, pictured here at the James Beard Awards in 2025, is among Chicago food industry leaders putting emphasis on worker safety and retention. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Locally, Draper is encouraged by growing reports of restaurants like progressive mainstay Lula Cafe, modern Chinese restaurant Lao Peng You, Consignment Lounge, Japanese dining bar Kumiko, and hospitality group 16 On Center engaging workers more democratically where policy and culture discussions are concerned.</p><p>Such conversations are happening in the months of fallout and introspection following last fall’s federal immigration enforcement campaign, dubbed “Midway Blitz.” Immigration actions hit restaurants especially hard and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/immigration/2025/11/21/small-businesses-still-reeling-from-operation-midway-blitz" target="_blank" ><u>disrupted business</u></a> for many. Figuring out how to get vulnerable employees to and from work or deciding protocol if ICE shows up prompted honest discussions about workers’ personal safety concerns and solidarity, as was the case at Kumiko.</p><p>“I think one of the most important things to remember when we think about restaurants as professional ecosystems is the sustainability of our most important resource: the people,” said Julia Momosé, founder, chef and bartender of Kumiko. “Ensuring their comfortability, safety and growth is at the forefront of all decision making.”</p><p>Having come from intense fine-dining environments before opening Kumiko in 2018, Momosé learned that keeping toxicity out of her workplace starts at hiring. She asks prospective employees very specific questions, “getting as personal as they’re comfortable with,” to get at their wants, needs and non-negotiables beyond compensation.</p><p>She places heavy emphasis on continuing education and hopes to offer financial aid for sake sommelier certification, in line with making sure employees “feel proud of what they do everyday for work.” She has internalized her own power to make adjustments — be it tightening the menu or limiting reservations to set the collective up for success at a high standard. It seems to be paying off, as some Kumiko employees have left and come back or been with the restaurant for three or four years, she said.</p><p>Much of Momosé’s hope lies with younger generations, who come in with much higher standards of how they expect to be treated and paid and aren’t afraid to say so.</p><p>“Their non-negotiables are very self-aware,” she said. “Some say it’s soft, but I welcome it.”</p><p>Indeed, for an industry where systemic abuse has long thrived in darkness beneath a facade of hardened individualism, speaking up remains a worker’s most potent tool for now.<br></p>
entries.author: Maggie Hennessy | WBEZ

entries.title: Iceberg straight ahead! But don’t worry, there’s plenty of room in the life raft
entries.summary: <p>The man in the deep-sea diving gear invited me on board the submersible and, for a split-second, I thought: Is this safe? Should I really be doing this?</p><p>After all, the contraption was mostly glass, and we would be descending into a suffocating darkness before reaching the world’s most famous shipwreck.</p><p>But then I remembered, I’d been advised a little earlier to simply relax and fully embrace the experience.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Participants have the sensation of being in a three-dimensional world, even though you’re actually wandering around a giant warehouse wearing a virtual reality headset</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>I climbed aboard. The actual Titanic sank 141 years ago on April 15.</p><p>Yes, “Titanic: A Voyage Through Time” is realistic — no, that’s not quite the right word.</p><p>But you do have the sensation of being in a three-dimensional world, even though you’re actually on dry land, wearing a virtual reality headset as you traipse through a giant gallery space in the Old Town neighborhood.</p><p>When the ocean rolls, you may instinctively flinch and reach for a handrail that isn’t there. Or when you are transported to Southampton, England on April 10, 1912, where thousands of well-wishers line the docks, you may hesitate, nervously eyeing the gangplank leading to the doomed passenger ship and wonder, Is that sturdy enough to support my weight?</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“The stairs are always the weird one for people — when you’re going up or down the staircase in the ballroom, it can be a little challenging if you’re not used to virtual reality,” said Parker Lee, the assistant site manager for the Titanic experience.</p><p>“Titanic” is expected to open later this month at 108 W. Germania Place. A ticket for the 45-minute adventure starts at $24.99 for adults and $19.99 for students, seniors and children 10 and up (the VR headsets aren’t designed for little kids). For more information, go to <a class="Link" href="http://titanicexperiencevr.com/chicago" target="_blank" ><u>titanicexperiencevr.com/chicago</u></a>. The experience is presented in English and Spanish.</p><p>Guests start out exploring the wreck, and then they are led — by members of the crew — to different parts of the ship, both above and below deck, as it makes its way toward disaster.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c40000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago Sun-Times reporter Stefano Esposito climbs a ladder on the virtual Titanic. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Titanic” offers a “powerful and deeply human journey through one of history’s most compelling maritime stories,” according to the promotional material.</p><p>Deeply moving? For those people whose hearts raced and eyes misted over during James Cameron’s 1997 epic, you may find yourself yearning for Leonardo DiCaprio’s cocky hero Jack Dawson leaning into the wind on the ship’s prow and yelling, “I’m the king of the world!”</p><p>And when you’re escorted into your VR first-class cabin, you won’t find a disrobed Rose (Kate Winslet’s character in the movie), reclining so that you can sketch her likeness.</p><p>That’s because this Titanic’s experience puts the focus on the historical characters, including the ship’s captain and architect. But rather than listening to them boast about the ship’s innumerable safety features, I found myself staring at their oddly glowing faces and creepily cold eyes — none of which detracts from the experience because I know how this story ends.</p><p>The experience took about 15 months to put together, said Emma Shaffer, a project manager with Fever, the entertainment company behind <i>Titanic</i>.</p><p>“This is specifically something we wanted to get pretty historically accurate. So we worked from the original plans of the Titanic,” said Shaffer, who is based in New York.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago Sun-Times reporter Stefano Esposito is surrounded by VR id panels in the room as he tries out the experience called “Titanic : A Voyage Through Time.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Fever pulled in animators, screenwriters, programers, sound designers, among others, in a team of 30 people.</p><p>The experience works best when you’re transported, virtually speaking, to the Belfast shipyard and you stare up at the ship’s colossal hull as it’s transformed — like a time-lapse movie from a skeletal frame to a steel-clad behemoth. Or when you’re dockside preparing to board the ship and you’re given a stern-to-bow view of the White Star Line’s pride and joy.</p><p>The show falls a bit flat when you’re in the oddly sterile dining room. It was at that point that I wished one of the helpful real-life staff members would approach and gently poke a caviar-topped cracker in my mouth.</p><p>What’s also missing, in my experience, was something to tickle the nostrils, like the scent of sea air perhaps. They’re working on that, Shaffer said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-db0000" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b933c60/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F61%2F5a4efa7040bea8867ac0c269a053%2Ftitanicvr-260409-01.jpg" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The experience allows for a maximum group size of six. The venue can accommodate up to 25 people navigating the VR ship at a time. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Participants are encouraged to wander around the deck, the boiler room and other parts of the ship. And while I never ran into any walls or other people, I took mostly timid steps — just in case. A VR grid appears to warn you of an approaching wall.</p><p>I was basically roaming alone, except when Chicago Sun-Times photographer Anthony Vasquez joined me exploring the wreck of the Titanic. So there was little danger of bumping into other people, who appear as blue avatars when they’re participating. The experience allows for a maximum group size of six, and the venue can accommodate up to 25 people navigating the VR ship at one time.</p><p>The experience concludes with, you guessed it, the ship plowing into an iceberg and all of the overly confident ship’s officers running around in a panic.</p><p>I was relieved to find myself — all but alone, as it turns out — in a lifeboat, which only seems fair given that I needed to get back to shore to write this story.<br></p>

entries.title: What can homeowners do to reduce flooding?
entries.summary: <p>Blamed in part on climate change, intense storms are happening <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/10/flooding-chicago-climate-change-deep-tunnel-mold-metropolitan-water-reclamation-district-soaked" target="_blank" ><u>more frequently and</u></a> dumping more rain, increasing the risk of flooding.</p><p>These storms can drop more than 8.5 inches of rain in 24 hours, flooding basements and causing billions of dollars in damage. Chicago’s sewers can handle just 2 inches before a flood becomes likely. State researchers predict that could increase by another inch by 2050.</p><p>Chicago sewers contain stormwater and wastewater, anything that you flush down your toilets or sinks at home. When rain falls fast and hard, some homes get that raw sewage gurgling up through basement drains.</p><p>But there are simple things we can all do to reduce flooding around our homes, neighborhoods and even across the city.</p><h3>Redirect the downspout</h3><p>The No. 1 recommendation from conservation, environmental and government sources is to redirect the downspout that pours water from your home’s rooftop gutters directly into the sewer. The idea is to cut the end off, add an extension and send that water to a garden or other landscaping</p><p>“Downspouts directly connect water from roofs to the sewer system, so during heavy rains the rain from all the roofs in an area reduces capacity in the sewer system,” and that can contribute to sewer backup and flooding in basements, says Cyatharine Alias, urban resilience director at Center for Neighborhood Technology.</p><p>It’s a relatively cheap and easy fix.</p><p>If you do it yourself, use a hacksaw or another cutting tool to shorten the downspout. <a class="Link" href="https://www.treepeople.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/How-to-Install-a-Downspout-Extension.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>Then attach an extension</u></a>, which is widely sold at hardware and big box home improvement stores. YouTube and TikTok how-to videos can provide guidance.</p><p>Of course, be careful to not point the water toward your neighbor’s property.</p><h3>Rain barrels</h3><p>Another way to prevent rainwater from seeping into your home is to use a rain barrel, a container that can fit under a gutter downspout. The 50 or more gallons of water captured can be used for irrigation. You can purchase a rain barrel from a number of <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/dam/city/depts/doe/general/NaturalResourcesAndWaterConservation_PDFs/Water/WheretoPurchase2010_9.8.10.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>hardware or garden stores</u></a>.</p><p>Or, if you live in Cook County, you can <a class="Link" href="https://mwrd.org/community-action/rain-barrels" target="_blank" ><u>buy directly</u></a> from the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago, the government body that manages stormwater and wastewater. The price per barrel is $22.30 or $11.15 for those 65 and older, according to the district.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/29389b0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1680x943+0+159/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F43%2Fcc%2F65ccb9464bfca5e68fbd11b9b388%2Fdownload-1.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Flooding is an increasing threat to homes in Illinois. There are many things people can do to help prevent flooding in their homes and in their neighborhood.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Mohammad Samra/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Replace hard surfaces </h3><p>Reducing the amount of hard surfaces, including concrete, around your property is another way to reduce the chance of basement flooding.</p><p>That water has to go somewhere, as the old saying goes. Pick water-absorbent surfaces like pavers or bricks with spaces between for backyard patios and walkways.</p><h3></h3><h3>Consider a “rain garden” or other native landscaping</h3><p>Go green, really green. Introduce native plants, such as a black-eyed Susan, purple coneflower or rye grass, in your backyard that can drink up the excess water. Make sure the landscaping is allowing water to flow away from your home as well as your neighbor’s.</p><p>Adding a rain garden full of native plants can help make your garden more beautiful while also absorbing the excess water and reducing the risk of flooding. Read more about how to create a rain garden<a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagobotanic.org/plant-information/conservation/rain-garden" target="_blank" ><u> here: </u></a></p><p>Of course, clear your gutters and make sure street sewer grates around your property aren’t blocked with leaves or other debris.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Reducing the amount of hard surfaces, including concrete, around your property can help reduce the chance of basement flooding.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Mohammad Samra/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Conserve water during storms</h3><p>Friends of the Chicago River and others suggest conserving water on storm days.</p><p>If you can avoid doing laundry or a lot of dishes when the rain is pounding down, it can save more space in the outdated sewers.</p><p>The reason we flood in Chicago is because the old sewer system is designed to catch 2 inches of rain in a 24-hour period.</p><p>Just three years ago, the Chicago area was getting four times that rain in 24-hours.</p><p>Here are <a class="Link" href="https://cnt.org/sites/default/files/uploads/RainReady-Capturing-Rainwater_0.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>more tips</u></a> from the Center for Neighborhood Technology, which has spent a lot of effort over the years on flood mitigation:</p><p><i>This article was produced as a project for USC Annenberg’s Center for Health Journalism and Center for Climate Journalism and Communication 2025 Health and Climate Change Reporting Fellowship.</i><br></p>
entries.author: Brett Chase

entries.title: Now that Bulls have captured a second first-round pick, what can they do?
entries.summary: <p>It might prove to be the best move of the Arturas Karnisovas Era, and it took 1,691 days to show itself.</p><p>Thanks to the Lauri Markkanen three-way trade to Cleveland on Aug. 27, 2021, Karnisovas — the recently fired Bulls executive vice president of basketball operations — acquired a first-round pick from Portland that was protected through 2028, when it would've conveyed as a second-round pick.</p><p>Thanks to the Trail Blazers winning Tuesday night’s play-in game against the Suns, however, the deal finally paid out, as the Bulls will likely have the No. 15 pick to go along with sitting at No. 9 for the draft lottery.</p><p>So what could that Portland pick look like in a loaded 2026 draft class? Very good.</p><p>Three possible prospects at No. 15:</p><p><b>Braylon Mullins – UConn – SG/SF</b></p><p>Unless the Bulls hit big with their first pick and jump into the top four (20.3% chance to do so), a run on guards will likely be the choice at No. 9 – think Mikel Brown Jr. That allows this second pick to be more luxury or need and every team needs shooting. Hello Mr. Mullins.</p><p><b>Aday Mara – Michigan – C</b></p><p>The 7-foot-3 big man is on the climb, showing in the championship run he can see the floor like a guard. Mara is a good rim-runner, and a legit protector in the paint. He could be an instant cover-up for all the issues the Bulls have had in the shot-blocking department at the center position.</p><p><b>Jayden Quaintance – Kentucky – C/PF</b></p><p>Before blowing his knee out at Arizona State his freshman season, the 6-foot-10 athletic big was holding block parties on a nightly basis (2.6 blocks per game). He transferred to Kentucky in what was really a season of recovery, but the feeling is he could be one of the bigger steals and worth the gamble for a team with multiple first-round picks.<br></p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

Employee abuse in Chicago’s hospitality industry isn’t exactly a secret. Being part of a high-turnover industry built on relationships, workers have little incentive to speak up if something untoward happens.</p><p><b>At issue</b>: Restaurants remain hierarchical small businesses with few formal systems for employee complaints. Power imbalances between workers and higher-ups are exacerbated by a tipped wage structure and by blurry lines of conduct. For survivors, paths toward safety, healing and accountability are slow-moving and routinely unsatisfying.</p><p><b>Working for better</b>: At least two grassroots organizations are trying to advocate for hospitality workers, offer mental health support and create ways to lodge formal complaints: Survivors Know; and Chicago Hospitality Accountability & Advocacy Database, or CHAAD. And a growing number of restaurants are engaging workers over internal policy and culture.</p><p><b>No quick fixes</b>: "Sometimes there is no justice. It's just removing yourself from the harm," said an ex-Warlord employee and longtime hospitality worker. Said Raeghn Draper, CHAAD executive director, "If your boss gets a whiff of you filing a complaint with an enforcement agency, you can get fired ... and there's not much to do in an at-will state."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-u/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>CHICAGO LIFE ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" couple households increase more than 70% in Chicago since 2005</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-b/" target="_blank" >Araceli Gómez-Aldana</a></h5><p><b>National trend</b>: New U.S. Census Bureau data shows same-sex couple households in the U.S. went up 78% since 2005, with most growth happening in the last 10 years. The survey uses American Community Survey one-year estimates for all years between 2005 and 2024. It shows about 1.4 million same-sex couple households in the U.S. in 2024.</p><p><b>Chicago POV</b>: The two community areas with the highest percentage of same-sex couple households are a section including Uptown, Edgewater and Rogers Park, followed by the Lake View and Lincoln Park area, according to the data. Same-sex couples make up 1.7% of all couples in Chicago and about 0.75% in the rest of Illinois.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: The rise in the count of same-sex couple households nationwide could reflect a more accurate representation. But experts point to various factors that can contribute to the data jump, from the evolution of census survey methods to the legalization of same-sex marriage.</p><p><a class="Link" class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-p/" target="_blank" >What homeowners can do to reduce flooding</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-x/" target="_blank" >Brett Chase</a></h5><p><b>Flooding frequency</b>: Blamed in part on climate change, intense storms are happening <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-c/" target="_blank" >more frequently</a> and dumping more rain, increasing the risk of flooding. Chicago’s sewers can handle just 2 inches before a flood becomes likely. Sewers contain stormwater and wastewater. When rain falls fast and hard, some homes get that raw sewage gurgling up through basement drains. </p><p><b>What you can do</b>:</p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-7b2f5003-38cf-11f1-9b0d-21ee60825983"><li><b>Redirect the downspout</b>: Reroute the downspout that pours water from your home’s rooftop gutters directly into the sewer by cutting the end off, adding an extension and sending that water to a garden or other landscaping.<br> </li><li><b>Rain barrels</b>: The container can fit under a gutter downspout, and the 50 or more gallons of water captured can be used for irrigation. <br> </li><li><b>Replace nonporous surfaces</b>: Reducing the amount of nonporous surfaces, including concrete, around your property is another way to reduce chances of basement flooding. Pick water-absorbent surfaces like pavers or bricks with spaces between for backyard patios and walkways.</li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-q/" target="_blank" ><b>MORE TIPS HERE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>MORE NEWS YOU NEED 🗞️</h4><div Vazquez/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-g/" target="_blank" >Chicago’s delivery robots and the debate over them</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-w/" target="_blank" >Elleiana Green</a></h5><p><b>Beep boop</b>: As companies push to dispatch more delivery robots across Chicago neighborhoods, some residents are fighting their deployment, part of an ongoing debate about the electric couriers. As of last month, there are 100 delivery robots running in the city. They mostly deliver food.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Tech companies Coco Robotics and Serve Robotics have rolled out delivery robots across Chicago over the last two years. Coco’s began appearing in the 27th and 34th wards. Serve launched 50 robots across much of the North and West sides last September. The robots were first allowed by the city in 2022, when then-Mayor Lori Lightfoot ushered in a City Council-approved Personal Delivery Device pilot program. The program will not renew past May 2027 without action from the council.</p><p><b>Forget a bot it?</b>: A CoCo Robotics delivery robot <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-yd/" target="_blank" >shattered the glass</a> of an Old Town bus shelter March 24, a day after a video of a Serve Robotics delivery robot rolling through the shattered glass of the <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-yh/" target="_blank" >bus shelter it destroyed in West Town</a> went viral earlier the same week. Nearly a month later, Serve Robotics put up a <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-yk/" target="_blank" >new ad to publicly apologize</a> for the damage.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-yu/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p><b>Should Chicago continue to have delivery robots on the city's streets? Tell us why or why not.</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply to this newsletter</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>ON WBEZ 91.5 FM 📻</h4><h4></h4><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-jr/" target="_blank" ><b>In the Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons, 9 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-7b2fc531-38cf-11f1-9b0d-21ee60825983"><li><b>What's next for Rainbow PUSH</b>: Yusef Jackson, youngest son of the late Rev. Jesse Jackson Sr., has just taken the helm of the civil rights organization his father founded. The new CEO of Rainbow PUSH talks about the future.<br></li><li><b>Protecting women</b>: Guests Delilah Martinez of the Mural Movement and the "Protect Women, Period" campaign, and Tio Hardman of Violence Interrupters, discuss a citywide campaign that's a response to incidents of violence on the CTA.</li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-jy/" target="_blank" ><b>Say More with Mary Dixon and Patrick Smith, 10 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-7b2fc534-38cf-11f1-9b0d-21ee60825983"><li><b>SNAP cuts</b>: Guest Danielle Perry of the Greater Chicago Food Depository talks about working to fill the gaps for 250,000 Illinoisans set to lose their SNAP benefits May 1.<br></li><li><b>Runner says goodbye</b>: Joabe Barbosa, a Brazilian national and social media star who strove to run every block in Chicago, must leave the U.S. as his visa is expiring. He'll share why he loves Chicago and exercising. Callers can weigh in, too.</li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-jj/" target="_blank" ><b>LISTEN LIVE 🎧</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>FROM THE PRESS BOX 🏀⚾🏒</h4><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-7b2fc537-38cf-11f1-9b0d-21ee60825983"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-jt/" target="_blank" ><b>Azurá Stevens returns</b></a>: The forward-center, a key piece of the Sky’s 2021 WNBA championship team, is back where the fit — and the money — made sense, writes Alissa Hirsh.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-ji/" target="_blank" ><b>The Grant Taylor vision</b></a>: The White Sox say they're not taking their prized righty's versatility for granted — or stretching him out for traditional starts.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-jd/" target="_blank" ><b>Offseason plans</b></a>: While missing the last five weeks of the season recovering from injury, Blackhawks rookie Oliver Moore talked about how hockey is played at this level. class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-ju/" target="_blank" >Heray Spice creates opportunities in Afghanistan with saffron</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-tl/" target="_blank" >Amy Yee</a></h5><p>Mohammad Salehi opens a jar of saffron and smells the dark crimson threads nestled inside. The spice has a rich, musky aroma that’s a reminder of home. Heray Spice, Salehi’s fair trade business, imports saffron grown by farmers in Herat in western Afghanistan, where he grew up.</p><p>Saffron, known as the world’s most expensive spice, lends its vibrant yellow hue to dishes such as paella, seafood and bouillabaisse. During Nowruz, the Persian New Year, many Afghans drink saffron tea and eat tahdig, a crispy rice dish with saffron, yogurt and butter. This year, Nowruz started March 20 and ended April 2.</p><p>Salehi started Heray Spice in 2017 with half a pound of saffron from his relatives’ farms in Herat. Now it sources from nearly 300 farmers and employs eight people in Afghanistan and six at its office near Lake View.</p><p>"Every time I hear from farmers in Herat about saffron, it’s deeply emotional for me," Salehi said. "It’s not just an agricultural crop; it represents community, dignity and hope."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-tr/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>WATCH: INSIDE HERAY SPICE ▶️</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="113" height="200" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/JYgiGxmk5oM?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Lake View's spice store creates opportunities in Afghanistan with saffron"></iframe> </div> </div></div><hr><p> </p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-tt/" target="_blank" ><b>How do you feel about recent statements from President Donald Trump and Pope Leo XIV related to the Iran war?</b></a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>"Trump is being honest this time. MAGA does not support the tenets of Christianity. He is showing who they worship." — <i>Howard Heath</i></p><p>"Pope Leo needs to stay out of politics. Trump is trying to bring stability to the Middle East by taking down the leadership in Iran. Trump is pro-life, which the Pope should praise." — <i>Robert Pomplun</i></p><p>"Trump’s statements about the Pope seem to be based on the false assumption that the Pope is a political figure. He doesn’t appear to understand that the Pope is a spiritual leader with a set of values and beliefs that are rooted in faith, humility and compassion. It will be interesting to see how this plays out." — <i>Jonathan Copulsky</i> </p><p>"As a Lutheran, I stand with the Pope. Conservatives have a long history of co-opting the language of Christianity for votes without acting on those values ... I hope American Catholics and Christians at large don't brush this off." — <i>Phoebe Murtagh</i></p><p>"I am horrified by Trump's statement about obliterating Iran off the face of the earth and ending a civilization! His rhetoric is violent, disrespectful and not acceptable! Pope Leo is reminding us to be peacemakers, and I totally agree with him." — <i>Lizz Verbeten</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia<br><b>Hat tip</b>: Sun-Times’ Joel Carlson for today’s subject line, which you’ll find on the <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-ti/" target="_blank" >front page of today’s newspaper</a> — on newsstands and online now.</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiihhjt-l-td/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>

entries.title: West Suburban Medical Center reopening some clinics amid eviction fight
entries.summary: <p>West Suburban Medical Center in Oak Park started resuming its hospital-based clinics and testing services in a “graduated manner” on Wednesday, according to the hospital’s owner.</p><p>Dr. Manoj Prasad, the owner of West Suburban and its private parent company Resilience Healthcare, said in a statement the hospital is resuming primary care clinics first and will soon start opening specialist clinics.</p><p>West Suburban’s main campus in Oak Park <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/03/25/west-suburban-medical-center-closing-runs-out-cash-weiss-memorial" target="_blank" ><u>closed suddenly late last month</u></a> because employees couldn’t be paid amid complications with the medical center’s records system. Prasad said it was a temporary closure and later pledged to reopen by summer.</p><p>The return of outpatient services comes a day after Prasad sued his business partner who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/04/14/west-suburban-medical-center-river-forest-campus-eviction-manoj-prasad" target="_blank" ><u>issued eviction notices</u></a> for all of Resilience Healthcare's hospital properties. The five-day notices demanded Prasad pay nearly $25 million in back rent.</p><p>“Getting the clinic back open and getting patients scheduled is our first priority,” Prasad said. “We are slowly bringing staff back and rebuilding operations step by step.”</p><p>Patients are being contacted by phone and email to schedule appointments. Some employees are returning after being furloughed, but a spokesperson for Prasad said the exact number of returning staff was still being determined.</p><p>The general surgery clinic is expected to resume next week. The emergency room and inpatient services remain closed. Outpatient clinics are only reopening at the Oak Park campus at this time.</p><p>According to the statement, resuming outpatient care is possible because of revenue regained after the hospital's electronic medical record system failed to properly bill for services. The hospital initiated a plan to get caught up on about 120,000 outstanding billing claims.</p>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: Gut-check time for coach Billy Donovan as the Bulls await his decision
entries.summary: <p>Billy Donovan is go-by-the-gut type of guy.</p><p>It’s more than worked for him, whether it was to stay at Providence and continue playing for a young coach named Rick Pitino, walk away from a Wall Street job right out of college and pursue coaching, or turn down an Orlando head coaching job 15 years ago after initially agreeing to it.</p><p>It’s a feel for Donovan and one that has a great track record, taking him on a journey to multiple college National Championships as the coach at Florida all the way to the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame.</p><p>Trust the gut.</p><p>So when the coach was talking in Dallas Sunday night after the Bulls wrapped up the season 31-51 and seemingly a few years away from even getting back to play-in game status, it was difficult to get a read from him and his decision to stay the Bulls coach or walk away.</p><p>Before the firing of executive vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas and general manager Marc Eversley last week, the Sun-Times was reporting that Donovan was likely gone. How gone? Put it at a solid 90%.</p><p>In the wake of those front office changes, it felt like it dropped to 49% gone.</p><p>Not even because there was major friction between Donovan and his two front office bosses, as much as it was Karnisovas and Eversley having no real direction or plan in place that left Donovan feeling like he could compete for something meaningful.</p><p>An idea that Donovan actually could have drawn an opinion on early on.</p><p>According to a source, Karnisovas and Eversley’s talent evaluation was questionable right away, going back to the 2020 draft – the duo’s first bite at the apple.</p><p>Holding No. 4, Karnisovas settled on Patrick Williams. There were multiple scouts and other personnel in the draft room, however, that wanted Tyrese Haliburon. Now, the entire draft process was messed up because of Covid, but Karnisovas specifically was not only fixated on Williams, but didn’t even consider Haliburton a serious prospect.</p><p>The source indicated that Karnisovas didn’t even feel like Haliburton would be a top four talent on the Bulls roster at the time.</p><p>Before his Achilles injury in the NBA Finals last season, there was an argument to be made that Haliburton was a top four talent in the entire Eastern Conference.</p><p>Not a good first step for the front office to provide Donovan with talent, and it never really got better from there.</p><p>Because that’s where Donovan is in his career.</p><p>He coaches to win games and compete. There is no fame or legacy being chased down at this point.</p><p>In the wake of the front office purge, Donovan wanted to have a sit-down with both Jerry and Michael Reinsdorf to get a feel of their vision and did so on Tuesday. He returned home to Florida to mull over everything with the expectation that an answer would come quickly.</p><p>“I don’t want to make excuses,” Donovan said. “Organizationally, Jerry and Michael, Arturas and Marc, and myself, we didn’t win as much as we wanted to. That’s just the bottom line. There’s a lot of things maybe you can point to, but the reality is we all share in that.</p><p>“You’re coming to an organization with an incredible fan base and great support, an organization that has won obviously a significant amount of NBA championships. You feel a responsibility to be able to win at the highest level and that did not happen. I always try to look through my own personal lens of how could I in some way done more or done better with the guys we had?”</p><p>The fact that Donovan left Chicago and returned home this week without an answer, however, says something.</p><p>Best guess, it feels like 60% Donovan is gone now.</p><p>Was something said or leaked? Nope.</p><p>Just trusting the gut.</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Mayor Johnson's City Council allies snuff out attempt to override subminimum wage veto
entries.summary: <p>The hourly pay of Chicago’s tipped workers while rise again to match the cost of living after the City Council failed to override <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/25/brandon-johnson-veto-freeze-subminimum-wage-phase-out-tipped-workers-restaurants" >Mayor Brandon Johnson’s third veto.</a></p><p>One month after freezing the phase-out of the subminimum wage at 76% to throw an economic lifeline to struggling Chicago restaurants, the Illinois Restaurant Association and its City Council allies did not pick up a single convert. Wednesday’s vote to override Johnson’s veto was 30-19 — four votes short of what's needed to override a mayoral veto.</p><p>Johnson has now issued three vetoes in three years and made all of them stick. The first killed a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/07/16/snap-curfew-veto-upheld-mayor-johnson-city-council-vote" >snap curfew ordinance. </a>The second <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/02/18/hemp-ban-brandon-johnson-veto-override-attempt-defeated-chicago-city-council" >killed a proposed ban</a> on the sale of most hemp-derived products in Chicago, and the third snuffed out efforts to freeze the hourly pay of restaurant servers, bartenders and other tipped workers at 76% pf the minimum wage.</p><p>It was a sorely needed political victory for a mayor who has struggled to pass his last two city budgets and had a City Council majority reject his corporate head tax and pass an alternative budget over his objections.</p><p>“It’s a victory for working people who need to have fair wages so they can afford to live in the city and stay in Chicago,” senior mayoral adviser Jason Lee said Wednesday. “At a time when people are struggling to make ends meet, lowering people's wages by potentially thousands of dollars a year would be counterproductive, and the mayor has advocated that we not do that.”</p><p>A fundamental axiom in politics is never to call for a vote that can't be won. Proponents of the freeze ignored that advice and tried for an override even though they knew they had no chance of succeeding.</p><p>“It’s to look restaurant owners in the eye and say, `We fought for you and we’re going to continue to fight for you,’ and say, `Just so we’re clear, these people over here don’t care about you,’“ said Ald. Matt O’Shea (19th), whose Far Southwest Side ward borders suburbs with restaurants that pay lower wages. “Maybe that puts a little pressure on people to move away from the mayor.”</p><p>Illinois Restaurant Association President Sam Toia targeted nine alderpersons who voted against the freeze despite having heavy concentrations of restaurants in their wards, in hopes of convincing at least four of them to change their votes and override the mayor’s veto.</p><p>None changed their votes.</p><p>When he issued the veto, Johnson argued that there has “never been a right time ever to roll back the rights and gains of women — particularly women of color,” but it’s "especially tone deaf and disturbing” now in the midst of an affordability crisis.</p><p>“Families are struggling under the weight of rising costs, rent, groceries, child care — and wages have not kept up with these rising costs. At a time when people are fighting just to stay afloat, you had individuals who claim to stand up for working people, as Democrats take money out of the pockets of working people,” the mayor said.</p><p>The Illinois Restaurant Association has spent the last year building support to stop the five-year phaseout and freeze the so-called “tip credit” at 24% of Chicago’s minimum wage, which now stands at $16.60 an hour.</p><p>Without the freeze, tipped workers now paid $12.62 an hour would receive a raise to 16% of Chicago’s minimum wage. That amount is reset every July 1 to match the cost of living.</p><p>With the freeze, they would still receive a raise, but it would be capped at 76% of whatever the minimum hourly wage turns out to be.</p><p>Restaurants are mandated by law to make up the difference for servers whose hourly pay and tips do not equal the minimum wage. Economic Development Committee Chair Gilbert Villegas (36th) argued that “99.9%” of Chicago restaurants are already complying with that law. </p><p>Villegas argued that Washington D.C. Mayor Muriel Bowser has reversed field on the issue, and that Chicago should do the same at a time when skyrocketing property taxes and costly city mandates like paid time off and sick time have been “piled on” to Chicago restaurants that were told to “figure it out.”</p><p>“They have figured it out — by reducing hours and staff,” Villegas said.</p><p>Ald. Anthony Beale (9th) urged his colleagues to “do the right thing, not the politically correct thing.” That prompted Ald. Jesse Fuentes (26th) to say, “Making sure that people can afford their rents and pay their bills and put food in their refrigerator is not a politically correct thing to do. It’s a human thing to do. It’s a just thing to do. It’s the right thing to do.”</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Mayor Johnson's push to have retired Ald. Walter Burnett lead CHA shut down by HUD
entries.summary: <p>Retired Ald. Walter Burnett — Mayor Brandon Johnson’s pick to lead the Chicago Housing Authority — is no longer eligible to become the CHA’s CEO.</p><p>The U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development denied the CHA’s request for conflict waivers for Burnett on Tuesday, marking the end of a seven-month stalemate with the federal agency.</p><p>Burnett’s apparent conflicts of interest were with his 30-year record as alderman and longtime ownership of properties rented to housing voucher holders, according to the CHA and HUD. Burnett and his wife have collected <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/09/12/alderman-walter-burnett-wife-rent-payments-cha-housing-voucher-landlords-hud" >more than $260,000</a> since 2007 as CHA voucher landlords.</p><p>Burnett cannot be appointed as the CHA’s leader since the retired alderman exercised “functions or responsibilities with respect to CHA for approximately 30 years,” according to a copy of a Tuesday letter from HUD obtained by the Sun-Times.</p><p>“CHA may not appoint Mr. Burnett to serve as the CEO within one year of his retirement from a position as a public official with the City of Chicago, or at any time while he or any immediate family member is participating as an owner in the” voucher program, Todd Thomas, HUD’s acting deputy assistant secretary of public housing and voucher programs, wrote. Burnett retired in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/09/05/mayor-brandon-johnson-walter-r-burnett-27th-ward-city-council" >August 2025</a>.</p><p>Mayoral spokesperson Griffin Krueger said in a statement that the mayor’s office is reviewing the HUD letter.</p><p>“There is clear prior precedent for individuals with Burnett’s background to assume executive leadership positions within public housing authorities across the country, this communication reaffirms that reality,” Krueger said.</p><p>Burnett, CHA interim Operating Chairman Matthew Brewer and HUD didn’t respond to requests for comment.</p><p>HUD’s letter, addressed to Brewer, is the latest development in what has been a tumultuous few weeks for Johnson and Brewer over the future of the agency. Their public skirmish escalated earlier this month when the mayor said he <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/01/mayor-johnson-oust-cha-board-chair-brewer-agency-vote-new-ceo" >removed Brewer</a> from his board chair and interim operation chairman role. It’s unclear if the mayor followed the proper procedures to do so. Brewer argues the mayor hasn’t.</p><p>Kruger said the mayor remains “committed to working with stakeholders to address the pressing concerns surrounding the anti-democratic procedure employed by the former Chairman which disenfranchised residents while stoking unnecessary confusion in this process.”</p><p>The CHA board voted 7-2 last month to appoint <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/03/17/chicago-housing-authority-appoints-keith-pettigrew-as-new-ceo" >Keith Pettigrew</a>, executive director of the District of Columbia Housing Authority, as its new CEO for a four-year term. He is set to start at the agency on Monday and will be tasked with righting a struggling agency that has been off course for well over a year.</p><p>Meanwhile, the mayor has remained steadfast in his support of Burnett, a mayoral ally who many CHA residents oppose, as the housing authority’s next CEO.</p><p>The agency's CEO selection committee — which included two representatives from the mayor’s team — recommended the mayor’s office interview three candidates, but the CHA was told the mayor wouldn’t schedule the interviews because he was recommending Burnett, according to Thomas’ letter.</p><p>The search committee then granted Burnett a “courtesy interview,” even though the retired alderman “had not applied for the position nor participated in the screening process with the executive search firm,” Thomas said.</p><p>“The Mayor’s Office was informed that Mr. Burnett was not selected as a finalist because of his limited operational experience managing” a public housing authority, Thomas said. “The selection committee also stated his background was inequivalent to the preferred finalists, resulting in the committee not selecting Mr. Burnett as a recommended finalist.”</p><p>Thomas said the mayor’s office still requested that the CHA seek a conflict-of-interest waiver from HUD for Burnett.</p><p>The CHA had been waiting on a final determination from staff at HUD’s headquarters in Washington, D.C. HUD’s letter said its regional office recommended that the waivers not be approved “because of failure to provide a good cause justification(s) for HUD to approve both waivers.”</p><p>The Sun-Times reported in November that Burnett was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/06/ex-ald-walter-burnett-chicago-housing-authority-ceo-candidates" >unlikely to lead the CHA</a>, as its board was moving forward with other candidates.</p><p>The CHA, which is the third-largest housing authority in the country, had been without a permanent leader since former CEO Tracy Scott resigned Nov. 1, 2024. The housing authority then launched a national search to find its next chief executive, saying it would appoint a new leader come summer 2025.</p><p>Housing advocates <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/04/10/advocates-file-lawsuit-against-chicago-housing-authority-over-appointment-of-new-ceo" >sued the CHA</a> in Cook County Circuit Court last week over its decision to hire Pettigrew, alleging the board’s vote didn’t follow the Open Meetings Act, which dictates how government agencies should conduct their public meetings. The housing authority has said it followed the law.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/01/mayor-johnson-oust-cha-board-chair-brewer-agency-vote-new-ceo" >Mayor Johnson looks to oust CHA board chair after agency votes in new CEO</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/03/17/chicago-housing-authority-appoints-keith-pettigrew-as-new-ceo" >Chicago Housing Authority appoints Keith Pettigrew as new CEO</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/08/08/mayor-brandon-johnson-walter-burnett-chicago-housing-authority-ceo-appointment-conflict-hud" >Retired Ald. Walter Burnett’s jump to top CHA post stalled</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Lizzie Kane

entries.title: Injured plover returns to Montrose Beach: 'Pippin is a trouper'
entries.summary: <p>A familiar friend returned Monday to the shores of Montrose Beach, according to the Chicago Park District and a group of birdwatchers.</p><p>Pippin, a Great Lakes Piping plover, has returned to the Chicago lakefront for his third year after hatching in 2023. The Chicago Piping Plovers also reported Monday that another plover, Imani, had returned to the lakefront, as well. He hatched there in 2021.</p><p>Last summer, Pippin left the lakefront with an injury to his foot. The Chicago Piping Plovers, a group of birdwatchers which began <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/04/11/piping-plovers-montrose-beach" target="_blank" >when the birds </a>were first spotted, said many feared the worst after his injury last year.</p><p>Pippin returned this week with a missing foot and subtle limp. Otherwise, the Chicago Park District said he is in good health. </p><p>“Pippin is a trouper,” said Irene Tostado, a spokesperson for the Chicago Park District, in a statement.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <blockquote class="bluesky-embed" data-bluesky-uri="at://did:plc:k33a6y5zoxkzmhobrj4hczls/app.bsky.feed.post/3mjftdw6thc2l" data-bluesky-cid="bafyreiazjgywih7zsdctgtdrr6n3sjsmdvtjdys2h5iaehtjzro5rshzmy"><p lang="en">Welcome home, Pippin! 🤍🟢 Our beloved Pippin has returned. Many will remember he departed last summer with something tangled around his foot. We feared the worst. Pippin has returned to us with his right foot missing and a limp but otherwise appears well. 📸:Liam Francis Shanley</p>— <a href="https://bsky.app/profile/did:plc:k33a6y5zoxkzmhobrj4hczls?ref_src=embed">Chicago Piping Plovers (@chicagopiping.bsky.social)</a> <a href="https://bsky.app/profile/did:plc:k33a6y5zoxkzmhobrj4hczls/post/3mjftdw6thc2l?ref_src=embed">2026-04-13T21:40:11.030Z</a></blockquote><script async src="https://embed.bsky.app/static/embed.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div></div><p>Pippin joins other <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/outdoors/2024/07/27/against-all-odds-piping-plovers-keep-making-history-in-illinois" target="_blank" >piping plovers — an endangered species — at Montrose Beach</a>. The 2,000 acres of nature are managed by the park system for their biodiversity and wildlife habitat.</p><p>“The presence of this endangered species is a testament to the importance of protecting our environment and wildlife through continuous conservation and sustainability efforts,” Tostado said in the statement. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"
