entries.author: Elleiana Green

entries.title: Tyler Bertuzzi's consistent scoring, battling made him bright spot among Blackhawks veterans
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> forward Tyler Bertuzzi doesn't show much of his unique personality in interviews, but he did joke a couple times this season about the long scoring droughts that plagued him in past seasons.</p><p>In early November, for example, he <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/11/12/blackhawks-tyler-bertuzzi-jeff-blashill-smooth-production-philipp-kurashev" target="_blank" >quipped</a> that he would probably turn to coach Jeff Blashill — one of his biggest supporters in the hockey world since their overlapping Red Wings eras — "when I don’t score for eight games or something."</p><p>Luckily for Bertuzzi, however, that long drought never really came. His longest was six games without a goal (or point) in mid-December. Last season, for contrast, he endured a 20-game goalless drought and another 12-game drought.</p><p>"Being more consistent is the big thing, and that's what I was this year," Bertuzzi said Tuesday. "The body felt good to be able to go out there every night and play to my best ability — to be able to forecheck and be hard to play against."</p><p>That consistency added up to a new career high of 32 goals (entering the season finale Wednesday against the Sharks), up from 23 goals last season. He also added 25 assists, up from 23 last season.</p><p>Almost all of his goals were scored within a few feet of the net, as has been the case throughout his nine-year career. He has carved out a niche as one of the NHL's best finishers in tight. Because of that, his 19.6% shooting percentage doesn't feel unsustainable.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">GOAL: Bedard to Bertuzzi to tie it on the 4-on-3! <a href="https://t.co/4Vky6YMG13">pic.twitter.com/4Vky6YMG13</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2032291240176132418?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 13, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>His emotional <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/01/04/blackhawks-tyler-bertuzzi-scoring-passion-golden-knights-jeff-blashill" target="_blank" >investment</a> in every game and dedication to doing the little things that go a long way was evident, too. Blashill described it well in January, saying that Bertuzzi has "played with that passion yet played in control." His ability to win puck battles and even strip the puck away from unsuspecting opponents stood out.</p><p>He was one of few veteran bright spots on the team. Fellow veteran forwards Ryan Donato, Teuvo Teravainen and Andre Burakovsky were considerably less impressive. At least Ilya Mikheyev was solid.</p><p>Bertuzzi also became an alternate captain after the trade deadline, and with two years left on the four-year contract he signed in 2024, he'll likely retain that role moving forward.</p><p>"Obviously production says he’s had a really good year, but to me, it’s way beyond that," Blashill said. "The only times he’s gotten himself in trouble in the past is trying to make too many plays. He ended up turning pucks over. He hasn’t done that at all this year. He’s been really good at making a play when there’s one there and living another day when there’s not."</p><h3><b>Not a skill</b></h3><p>Bertuzzi, Mikheyev and rookie Anton Frondell formed easily the Hawks' most effective line down the stretch. Entering Wednesday, they had together outscored opponents 7-3 during a time period in which the team was outscored 51-27 as a whole.</p><p>Bertuzzi's advice to Frondell to "go hunt, go forecheck" was clearly useful. But asked how the rest of the Hawks' many young forwards could learn that skill, Bertuzzi pushed back against that word usage.</p><p>"It's not a skill; it's just competing and wanting to win the battle," he said. "It's something that will grow with time."</p><h3><b>Contract watch</b></h3><p>Connor Bedard's contract <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/13/connor-bedard-new-blackhawks-contract-negotiation-cap-hit" target="_blank" >negotiations</a> will be a big deal this summer, but the Hawks do have a few other restricted free agents to also re-sign: Kevin Korchinski, Ethan Del Mastro and Drew Commesso.</p><p>Mikheyev, Matt Grzelcyk and Sam Lafferty are their pending unrestricted free agents. Mikheyev is the only one with a decent chance of returning.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Robot de entrega se ‘disculpa’ en nuevo anuncio tras romper el vidrio de un refugio de parada de autobuses
entries.summary: <p>Un anuncio atrevido —y a la vez arrepentido— para los robots de entrega ha aparecido en el refugio de parada de autobuses del vecindario de West Town, que fue dañado por uno de esos robots.</p><p>El anuncio llega casi un mes después de que un robot de entrega de Serve Robotics llamado <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/24/delivery-robot-goes-viral-after-shattering-west-town-bus-shelter-glass" target="_blank" >Nasir se estrellara contra ese refugio</a> cerca de las avenidas Racine y Grand, rompiendo un panel de vidrio.</p><p>“Queridos vecinos de West Town, tomé 'entrar al mercado' demasiado literal. Lamento mucho lo del refugio de autobuses... y la entrada dramática. Prometo hacerlo mejor”, dice el anuncio. Incluye una foto del robot avergonzado con una pantalla digital que dice “Nasir lo siente”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-230000" name="module-230000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Después del incidente, Serve Robotics trabajó con JC Decaux, la empresa de publicidad que construyó el refugio, para limpiar los daños. El refugio fue reparado en pocos días después del accidente.</p><p>“Retiramos el anuncio para disculparnos directamente con nuestros vecinos de West Town, aceptar el error y compartir el compromiso de Serve con la mejora continua de nuestra tecnología”, dijo Serve Robotics en un comunicado.</p><p>La empresa explicó que el accidente ocurrió después de que los tres sistemas de sensores del robot no detectaron la pared de vidrio transparente del refugio. Serve añadió que ha mejorado la forma en que el sistema interpreta entornos de vidrio y está actualizando su software y hardware de nueva generación para "hacer que la detección sea más confiable".</p><p>Aunque los incidentes son raros, "raro no es suficiente", dijo Serve. "Queremos que sea cero. Estamos trabajando estrechamente con la Ciudad de Chicago y compartiendo lo que hemos aprendido para que el sistema continúe mejorando en todos los lugares donde operamos".</p><p>Un incidente similar ocurrió la misma semana en Old Town, involucrando un robot de entrega de otra compañía, Coco Robotics. Ese <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/25/delivery-robot-smashes-into-old-town-bus-shelter-in-second-crash-of-the-week" target="_blank" >robot rompió el vidrio</a> de un refugio de autobuses en las calles Larrabee y North.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">A Serve Robotics delivery robot crashes into a CTA bus shelter in Chicago's West Town neighborhood, shattering the glass panel, March 2026. <a href="https://t.co/ZtGmOsRdku">pic.twitter.com/ZtGmOsRdku</a></p>— Future Adam Curtis B-Roll (@adamcurtisbroll) <a href="https://twitter.com/adamcurtisbroll/status/2036563059548852543?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 24, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Un <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/rquinnmyers/status/2036614748251226282?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw%7Ctwcamp%5Etweetembed%7Ctwterm%5E2036614748251226282%7Ctwgr%5Ec70601c467e0eac1de0d5551ea11cf89ab6bcbfa%7Ctwcon%5Es1_&ref_url=https%3A%2F%2Fblockclubchicago.org%2F2026%2F03%2F25%2Frobots-gone-wild-food-delivery-robots-smash-2-bus-shelters-in-chicago%2F" target="_blank" >video</a> de ambos accidentes se hizo viral en las redes sociales. Mientras algunos usuarios encontraron humor en los desafortunados incidentes, otros estaban preocupados por la seguridad de las personas.</p><p>Los opositores a los robots de entrega <a class="Link" href="https://nosidewalkbots.org/" target="_blank" >iniciaron una petición</a> el verano pasado para pedir a la Municipalidad que detuviera el programa de robots en las aceras hasta que se publicaran los hallazgos de seguridad sobre los dispositivos y se establecieran reglas sobre cómo se implementan. A partir del 27 de marzo, la petición recibió más de 4,000 firmas de más de 55 códigos postales de toda la ciudad.</p><p>Coco Robotics y Serve Robotics están <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2026/delivery-robots-chicago-food-coco-robotics" target="_blank" >operando robots en los lados norte y oeste de Chicago</a> bajo un <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/mayor/press_room/press_releases/2022/september/PersonalDeliveryDevicePDDPilotProgramPasses.html" target="_blank" >programa piloto</a> que el Concejo Municipal aprobó en 2022. Coco llegó a la ciudad en 2024, y Serve comenzó a implementar su flota en septiembre pasado.</p><p>Los robots de Coco son siempre monitoreados virtualmente por un humano, mientras que los de Serve en su mayoría se conducen solos, con humanos interviniendo cuando es necesario. El programa piloto ha enfrentado críticas.</p><p>El director ejecutivo de Serve Robotics, Ali Kashani, le dijo a WBEZ que está consciente de la oposición y que la empresa está abierta a interactuar con los habitantes de Chicago preocupados.</p><p>“Las personas pueden comunicarse con nosotros”, aseguró Kashani. “Cada correo electrónico recibe respuesta”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Council turns up heat to find replacement for ShotSpotter
entries.summary: <p>Opposition City Council members are demanding hearings to determine why Chicago is headed into yet another summer without the crime-fighting tool known as ShotSpotter.</p><p>Shortly after taking office, Mayor Brandon Johnson got rid of the controversial <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2024/09/13/shotspotter-contract-renewal-city-council-vote-rules-committee-rule-41-snelling-johnson" >gunshot detection system </a>he denounced as a “walkie-talkie on a stick” to honor a campaign promise to his progressive supporters who viewed the technology as costly, ineffective and a contributor to over-policing in Black and Brown communities where the majority of gun violence occurs.</p><p>A defiant City Council then empowered Chicago Police Superintendent Larry Snelling to sign a new contract with ShotSpotter, but Johnson declared the ordinance “illegal” — even after negotiating a costly extension to retain the technology through the 2024 Democratic National Convention in Chicago.</p><p>Johnson then launched an open competition to replace the gunshot detection technology. SoundThinking, the company that owns ShotSpotter, was one of eight firms that responded to the so-called “request-for-information” by the Sept. 20, 2024 deadline.</p><p>Nothing has happened since then — even though the last two city budgets set aside a combined $13.9 million for the replacement technology.</p><p>Now ShotSpotter’s leading proponents — Public Safety Committee Chair Brian Hopkins (2nd), Ald. Peter Chico (10th), a former Chicago police officer and Derrick Curtis (18th) — are turning up the heat.</p><p>They introduced a resolution at Wednesday’s City Council meeting to examine “delays and missed deadlines” to replace the acoustic gunshot detection system that was once an intrinsic part of Chicago’s crime-fighting technology.</p><p>The hearing would also examine what resolution sponsors call “protracted delays in implementing a records management system to improve data collection, analysis and officer oversight, including an early intention system to identify at-risk behavior.”</p><p>Hopkins argued that ShotSpotter saves lives, and said it is imperative that Chicago not go through another traditional summer crime surge without the technology aimed at speeding police response to shootings and apprehending those responsible.</p><p>“The whole point of it is to dispatch police automatically when shots are fired. This administration is philosophically opposed to that. So even though they said they would replace ShotSpotter, their preference would be for this whole issue to just fade away and we’re not gonna let that happen,” Hopkins told the Sun-Times.</p><p>“Lives are at stake here. We now have numerous documented cases where people were shot — in some cases fatally — in areas formerly covered by ShotSpotter’s footprint and no 911 call was made. The victim was found. In some cases, it was minutes. In some cases, it was hours. But in all cases, it was longer than would have been likely if ShotSpotter had triggered an alert.”</p><p>Curtis accused Johnson of “slow-walking” the process for selecting a replacement technology, perhaps to avoid the political embarrassment of having to acknowledge that ShotSpotter was the best technology all along.</p><p>“They told us that they did have a competition” for a replacement technology, “but [it] was never disclosed who it was,” Curtis said. “He’s not going to replace it because he’d be going back on what he said he was going to do” during the campaign.</p><p>But Curtis added, “I’m hoping that enough of the Council that helped get rid of ShotSpotter see the need for it now. We’ve got ten months left. He may say that violent crime is down. Oh, yeah. Statistically it is. But tell that to the parent of a kid who was just killed.”</p><p>Garien Gatewood claims he was fired as deputy mayor for community safety by senior mayoral adviser Jason Lee and chief of staff Cristina Pacione-Zayas in apparent retaliation for a complaint he filed with the inspector general’s office in October accusing them of improper interference in City Hall’s contracting process.</p><p>The mayor has insisted that he was unaware before Gatewood was fired that he had filed the complaint about Lee and Pacione-Zayas. </p><p>Curtis said he would “love” to have Gatewood voluntarily agree to testify at the upcoming hearings “to clear some things up for us.” But Gatewood said he has no intention of attending the hearing and cannot be compelled to do so.</p><p>Lee argued that Chicago is getting along fine without ShotSpotter because the "fundamental fact" is that "crime is down. Shootings are down." </p><p>"What the mayor has said is there were flaws with the ShotSpotter technology, that ShotSpotter wasn't essential to safety and that's been born out. The average Chicagoan is looking at the results, how safe do they feel and they feel a lot safer since Mayor Johnson was elected," Lee said. <br>"If the goal is to reduce the number of people victimized in Chicago, then that goal has been achieved."</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: UNESCO International Jazz Day puts the spotlight on Chicago music in April: Here’s what to see
entries.summary: <p>On a single day later this month, jazz-lovers can hear a world premiere by Ernest Dawkins in Englewood, or a tribute to Randy Weston and Melba Liston in Hyde Park. On the West Side, they can enjoy a set by trumpeter Victor Garcia in Austin, or a retrospective of Chicago jazz at the Garfield Park Conservatory.</p><p>And that’s only a sample of shows on “Neighborhood Jazz Night,” which takes place April 28 as part of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/01/20/chicago-unesco-international-jazz-celebration-lineup-venues" target="_blank" ><u>UNESCO International Jazz Day</u></a> programming. Chicago is a first-time host for the celebration, which culminates in the All-Star Global Concert on April 30 at Lyric Opera House. </p><p>That performance will feature more than 40 artists, including Herbie Hancock, Kurt Elling, Dianne Reeves, Dee Dee Bridgewater and Terence Blanchard. And it will be live-streamed to hundreds of millions around the world.</p><p>But the large-scale show only captures some of Chicago's jazz scene. Happening all month, a wealth of events — many of them free — will explore the history of jazz in the city, honor<b> </b>homegrown talent and support local venues. Some highlights include a tribute to women in jazz at the Chicago History Museum, a celebration of the legacy of jazz radio in Chicago, and local hero Orbert Davis’ reinterpretation of “Sketches of Spain” by Miles Davis and Gil Evans. For a full schedule and ticket information, visit <a class="Link" href="http://chicagojazzalliance.org" target="_blank" ><u>chicagojazzalliance.org</u></a>.</p><p>“It's going to be such a thrill,” <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2025/10/02/kurt-elling-jazz-chicago-musician-grammy-broadway-hadestown-hermes" target="_blank" ><u>Kurt Elling</u></a> said. “The whole city is really coming alive.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-760000" name="image-760000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/743c8b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5696x3197+0+300/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fde%2F88bc56b74ae282f46def8d588987%2Ffreefest-091825-6.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d9a0910/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5696x3197+0+300/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fde%2F88bc56b74ae282f46def8d588987%2Ffreefest-091825-6.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d28a67d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5696x3197+0+300/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fde%2F88bc56b74ae282f46def8d588987%2Ffreefest-091825-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="FREEFEST-091825_6.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d28a67d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5696x3197+0+300/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fde%2F88bc56b74ae282f46def8d588987%2Ffreefest-091825-6.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/15c5eaa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5696x3197+0+300/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fde%2F88bc56b74ae282f46def8d588987%2Ffreefest-091825-6.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d28a67d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5696x3197+0+300/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fde%2F88bc56b74ae282f46def8d588987%2Ffreefest-091825-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago saxophonist, composer and educator Ernest Dawkins is a featured performer at UNESCO International Jazz Day events throughout April. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Sending a message of unity and harmony</h3><p>Elling, a Chicago native, is co-artistic director of the global concert alongside Herbie Hancock, another Chicagoan. Hancock is also a co-founder and chair of UNESCO International Jazz Day, which is observed in more than 190 countries. This year, the festivities coincide with <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/04/02/america-250-illinois-chicago-federal-national-endowment-arts-trump" target="_blank" ><u>America’s 250th anniversary</u></a> and Illinois native Miles Davis’ 100th birthday.</p><p>“The message of International Jazz Day is cross-cultural unity and American harmony,” Elling said. “We deserve to have the entire city come together to manifest this message and send it out far and wide.”</p><p>From veterans like Ernest Dawkins to younger stars like Marquis Hill, Elling said he is advocating for as much Chicago talent as possible to hit the stage at the global concert.</p><p>“Everybody from the cats who regularly appear at the Green Mill to the cats who are exemplary players when it's time for the Hyde Park Jazz Festival to the stand-up singers here in town,” he said. “I will also get to sing eight or ten bars — maybe 16 or 32 bars — of something.”</p><p>If Elling has his way, Chicago’s musical contributions will be spelled out in the script for the program. Jazz giants were not only born in Chicago, but some, like Louis Armstrong, migrated to the city and made seminal records here, he said.</p><p>“I want Chicago to have its moment,” he said. “I am going to work as hard as I can to wedge in as much Chicago music history as I can and, thankfully, there’s an incredible abundance.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-270000" name="image-270000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a3bfb4b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F09%2F95bd2ab0da7159399732dfdf0db5%2Fmerlin-101603966.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c4e34dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F09%2F95bd2ab0da7159399732dfdf0db5%2Fmerlin-101603966.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/57f7353/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F09%2F95bd2ab0da7159399732dfdf0db5%2Fmerlin-101603966.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Forum, located at 324 E. 43rd St. in the Bronzeville, is the city’s oldest assembly and social hall. In its heyday, it hosted some of the biggest names in Black history." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/57f7353/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F09%2F95bd2ab0da7159399732dfdf0db5%2Fmerlin-101603966.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4568c02/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F09%2F95bd2ab0da7159399732dfdf0db5%2Fmerlin-101603966.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/57f7353/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2c%2F09%2F95bd2ab0da7159399732dfdf0db5%2Fmerlin-101603966.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>As part of UNESCO International Jazz Day programming, attendees will get a tour of the historic Forum venue in Bronzeville, where Nat King Cole, bassist Milt Hinton and violinist and educator Walter Dyett once performed.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Bronzeville to become a ‘living’ jazz museum</h3><p>Enthusiasts will<b> </b>learn plenty of that history by attending<b> </b>“<a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagojazzalliance.org/events/attheforumbronzeville" target="_blank" ><u>At the Forum - The Bronzeville Roots of Chicago Jazz</u></a>,” on April 25. <a class="Link" href="https://www.theforumbronzeville.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Built in 1897</u></a> on East 43rd Street, the historic venue was a cultural hub for jazz musicians, including Nat King Cole, bassist Milt Hinton and violinist and educator Walter Dyett.</p><p>During the free event, visitors will get a sneak peek at the space, which is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/black-history/2022/2/1/22910969/bronzeville-forum-renovation-restoration-black-history-month-urban-juncture-bernard-loyd" target="_blank" ><u>currently being renovated</u></a>. The community-focused day will include food and music in The Forum Cafe; a tour of The Forum Hall and a gallery curated by the Black Metropolis Research Consortium; and a community walk to the former homes of Cole and Louis Armstrong, who lived in the area with his wife, Lil Hardin.</p><p>Essentially, the neighborhood will feel like a “living museum of jazz history,” according to organizers.</p><p>“Bronzeville is really the cultural and commercial heart of Black Chicago,” said Bernard Loyd, who owns The Forum and is renovating the property under his development firm, Urban Juncture. “We'll be engaging in a discussion about how this great gathering of folks shaped the evolution of jazz and the world.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div Day events. “Getting the narrative out there that these spaces are safe and you can come here and have a good time is important,” she said.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Alex Wroblewski/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>A beloved jazz club bus tour returns</h3><p>While The Forum Hall is still years away from re-opening, plenty of venues in the city are currently welcoming live performers every week. Audiences can support them by joining the Jazz Club Tour Experience on April 27.</p><p>Presented by the Jazz Institute of Chicago, the tour features two paths: The Miles Davis Route takes attendees to South Side clubs such as Parkway Social and Bronzeville Winery. The Melba Liston Route takes attendees to central-area clubs including Jazz Showcase and Winter’s Jazz Club. Performers along the tour include the Justin Dillard Organ Trio, Alysha Monique, Ernest Dawkins and alumni from the institute’s Jazz Links education program.</p><p>The event is a low-barrier way to get exposed to the jazz scene, said Heather Ireland Robinson, executive director of the Jazz Institute of Chicago, who helped organize the International Jazz Day programming.</p><p>Audience-building is crucial, as some clubs are still recovering from the COVID-19 pandemic, she added. And some patrons have expressed fears about traveling Downtown.</p><p>“Getting the narrative out there that these spaces are safe and you can come here and have a good time is important,” Robinson said. “And making sure those audiences are diverse — that it's not just people from the North Side, but that everybody comes and sees themselves reflected in the music.”</p><div class="Enhancement" entries.author: Erica Thompson

entries.title: LaTanya Long-Sullivan, Hyde Park crossing guard who was tiny, fierce and adored, dies at 64
entries.summary: <p></p><p></p><p></p><p>In a giant coat that nearly swallowed her four-foot-something frame, and holding a red stop sign that seemed half her size, LaTanya Long-Sullivan often received hugs from kids and parents, and pretty much any other regular passerby, before shepherding them across the street at the corner of 57th and Kimbark in Hyde Park.</p><p>"Ms. Tanya," as she was known to generations of Ray Elementary School and Chicago Lab School students, was a crossing guard at the corner for 26 years and was ingrained in the community. She attended graduation ceremonies, remembered names and birthdays and hardly ever missed a day of work.</p><p>"She had a huge personality and called all the kids her 'kids,'" said Michelle Jefferson, who has two kids who attended Ray. "And she was always very protective of the children."</p><p>Despite her small stature, she could immediately become fierce if a driver endangered safety at her corner.</p><p>"If drivers pushed the limits, she would yell if she needed to," Jefferson said.</p><p>Mrs. Long-Sullivan's brother, Kevin Long, said she learned how to be a guardian long before she picked up the stop sign.</p><p>"When we were kids, she wouldn't let nobody do nothing to her sisters or brothers. She was tiny, but she came in fighting, didn't do no talking. And if you was a bully on the block you couldn't bully us," he said.</p><p>Mrs. Long-Sullivan died March 30 from what family suspects was a heart attack. She was 64.</p><p>"She just loved hugs and happy children and excited parents, and she was humorous just had an unwavering commitment," said her sister, Diedre Long.</p><p>Mrs. Long-Sullivan grew up in South Shore, the middle child of five siblings. Her parents were Charlie and Dorothy Long. He was a mechanic and a gas station owner. She was a teacher's assistant.</p><p>Ray Elementary School students held a memorial last week near the corner where Mrs. Long-Sullivan performed her duties and put up poster boards that displayed messages from the students that included: "You are the best because you are kind" and "Thank you for your special gift" and "She was the best, if I had a wish I would bring you back to life."</p><p>Hundreds of students also placed flowers at the corner.</p><p>"She wasn't just a crossing guard. She was more like a mom," said Ray Principal Gayle Harris-Neely.</p><p>"It was beautiful to see the tribute," Harris-Neely said. </p><p>Mrs. Long-Sullivan kept little treasures — like dolls or toy cars — in her backpack to give to kids for birthdays and other special occasions, with their parents' permission, her sister said.</p><p>"She cared more about people than herself," she said. "Kids who graduated and were grown would come back to introduce their children to her because she made a difference in their lives."</p><p>Mrs. Long-Sullivan, who lived in South Shore, is survived by her husband, Clifford Long, and her son, Darrius Long.</p><p>A funeral service is scheduled for 11 a.m. Thursday at Gatling's Chapel, Inc., 10133 S. Halsted St.</p>
entries.author: Mitch Dudek

entries.title: Las inundaciones en Chicago están empeorando. Te explicamos el porqué.
entries.summary: <p>A principios del año pasado en un memorado poco notado, los científicos de Illinois hicieron una predicción alarmante.</p><p>"Boletín 76", una comunicación de investigadores de la Universidad de Illinois, advirtió que la lluvia intensa, agravada por el cambio climático, se volvería mucho más severa en los próximos 25 años.</p><p>"Lo que se considera seguro y adecuado hoy puede no ser cierto en el futuro", escribieron sobre la amenaza a personas, hogares y edificios.</p><p>La amenaza ha ido en aumento durante años. En el último siglo en Chicago, la probabilidad de tormentas de lluvia intensas se ha incrementado siete veces. Estas tormentas pueden dejar más de 8.5 pulgadas de lluvia en 24 horas.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-170000" name="module-170000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Diseñadas hace décadas, las alcantarillas de Chicago sólo pueden manejar 2 pulgadas en ese corto período de tiempo antes de que las inundaciones se vuelvan probables.</p><p>Eso significa que cada vecindario en Chicago corre el riesgo de inundaciones, y esa amenaza aumenta con cada gran tormenta.</p><p>Hace medio siglo, comenzó la construcción del llamado “<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2015/5/11/18615687/thornton-quarry-the-grand-canyon-of-the-south-suburbs-will-soon-be-underwater" target="_blank" >Deep Tunnel</a>” para mejorar el antiguo método de control de inundaciones de la ciudad: arrojar agua de alcantarilla directamente al Río Chicago, una práctica que continúa hoy, aunque con menor frecuencia.</p><p>El sistema de miles de millones de dólares de túneles subterráneos y enormes embalses diseñados para capturar el agua de inundaciones ha funcionado para ayudar a proteger el río, así como el Lago Michigan.</p><p>Pero no ha detenido los sistemas de alcantarillado de los vecindarios, que transportan tanto aguas pluviales como todo lo que se arroja por los inodoros, de retroceder hacia los sótanos de las casas a través de los desagües, destruyendo propiedades y creando condiciones insalubres.</p><p>Chicago fue construida sobre un pantano y siempre ha tenido problemas de inundaciones. Las tormentas eléctricas más fuertes, alimentadas en parte por el cambio climático, están abrumando las alcantarillas de la ciudad. La lluvia cae tan fuerte y tan rápido que el agua no tiene oportunidad de fluir hacia esas enormes tuberías construidas bajo el proyecto Deep Tunnel.</p><p>Las estimaciones de daños a hogares y otras propiedades en Chicago se han disparado a miles de millones de dólares en los últimos 20 años, según muestran los registros gubernamentales.</p><p>Pero el boletín de los científicos climáticos del año pasado no fue sólo una advertencia. Fue un llamado a la acción, uno que los políticos, planificadores e ingenieros pueden atender o enfrentar consecuencias catastróficas.</p><p>Para tener una idea de qué tan grave puede ser, considera lo que ocurrió en 2023, cuando las tormentas inundaron el lado oeste, el lado sur y suburbios cercanos con 8 a 9 pulgadas en un corto período.</p><p>Las alcantarillas fueron abrumadas, inundando docenas de miles de hogares y obligando a cientos de personas a buscar refugio en otros lugares. Se cree que muchas cientos, si no miles, todavía están viviendo con moho como resultado del agua que se filtró en su interior, entrando por desagües e incluso paredes de sótanos.</p><p>"Este es un problema grave de salud pública", dice Clabe Johnson, cuyo apartamento en el vecindario de Austin fue inundado con 4 pies de agua en julio de 2023.</p><p>Dice que su hermano Gene, que se quedaba con él, sufrió problemas respiratorios debido al moho persistente y fue hospitalizado.</p><p>"Nos sentimos impotentes y desamparados", añade.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>El moho cubre las paredes del sótano de una casa en el vecindario de Austin de Chicago. Muchas personas en la comunidad aún están lidiando con problemas de moho debido a las inundaciones de 2023.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Más lluvia, más problemas</h3><p>En todo el lado oeste, los defensores de la comunidad dicen que los residentes todavía son acosados por el moho de esa tormenta.</p><p>Aún más sobrevivientes de inundaciones en Chicago y el Condado de Cook todavía están esperando ayuda gubernamental después del diluvio de 2023.</p><p>"Sin cambios, veremos un aumento en los impactos [de desastres]", dice el climatólogo estatal de Illinois, Trent Ford, quien consultó para el memorando de 2025, que advirtió que la cantidad de lluvia que cae en 24 horas durante estas tormentas podría aumentar otra pulgada para 2050.</p><p>Incluso un aumento de un cuarto de pulgada de lluvia puede afectar a cientos de personas y causar millones de dólares en daños adicionales por inundaciones, dice Ford.</p><p></p><p>La clave para prevenir inundaciones, coinciden muchos expertos, involucra crear más espacios verdes para ayudar a absorber el exceso de agua antes de que llegue a las alcantarillas y construir más infraestructura subterránea para capturar o desviar el agua a otro lugar.</p><p>El desafío es que estas soluciones pueden tardar muchos años y costar millones, si no es que miles de millones de dólares.</p><p>Las ciudades en Illinois y en todo Estados Unidos ya están muy rezagadas en la carrera por contrarrestar las destructivas fuerzas de la naturaleza, dicen los expertos.</p><p>La infraestructura obsoleta, como alcantarillas y calles, y áreas urbanas densas y sobreconstruidas, planificadas hace décadas con demasiado concreto y muy pocos espacios verdes, convierten las tormentas importantes en eventos de inundación severa.</p><p>Con menos superficies permeables, el agua no tiene a dónde ir. El entorno construido es tanto culpable como el clima que se intensifica.</p><p>La gran mayoría de las personas que experimentan el mayor impacto se encuentran en comunidades afroamericanas y latinas, donde los edificios, calles y otra infraestructura han visto menos mejoras.</p><p>Estas áreas suelen presentar grandes extensiones de pavimento y otras superficies duras construidas sobre zonas bajas u otros lugares con mal drenaje y falta de espacios verdes.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" Jackson Blvd en Austin. Los propietarios aún enfrentan los daños causados por las inundaciones de 2023.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>‘La gente está traumatizada cada vez que llueve’</h3><p>En julio de 2023, Austin, el vecindario del lado oeste donde vive el alcalde Brandon Johnson, fue golpeado por una inundación histórica que abrumó la alcantarilla tras más de 8 pulgadas de lluvia, obligando a cientos a abandonar sus apartamentos en los sótanos y creando géiseres que incluso levantaron la tapa de una alcantarilla, según un testigo.</p><p>Ese fue solo un vecindario. Esa tormenta inundó al menos 70,000 sótanos del lado oeste y suburbios en el verano de 2023.</p><p>Y muchas personas se enfrentaron a la limpieza del desastre por su cuenta. El seguro privado generalmente no cubre los daños por inundaciones y la ayuda federal puede ser difícil de conseguir y a menudo se retrasa, lo que ha sucedido en Chicago.</p><p></p><p>La Municipalidad fue premiada con $426 millones el año pasado por el gobierno federal con "fines de recuperación de desastres" tras la inundación de 2023. Casi tres años después, ninguno de esos fondos ha sido gastado.</p><p>Eso es una terrible noticia, según Princess Shaw de North Lawndale, quien ha estado abogando por una ayuda rápida para los sobrevivientes de inundaciones del lado oeste que han estado esperando asistencia con la construcción y la eliminación de moho durante los últimos tres años.</p><p>"La gente está traumatizada cada vez que llueve", dice.</p><p>Shaw observa una falta de responsabilidad entre los políticos y burócratas de la Municipalidad y el estado y califica los esfuerzos del gobierno para abordar el problema como desarticulados: "Hay mucha búsqueda de culpables".</p><p>En una declaración escrita, Johnson culpa a los procedimientos para asegurar dinero federal. También destacó la necesidad de mejoras para reducir las inundaciones.</p><p>"Estos son mis vecinos y, como un residente del lado oeste, comparto su frustración con este proceso", asegura Johnson. "Las deficiencias de infraestructura que agravaron los efectos de esta tormenta reflejan décadas de falta de inversión sistémica en las comunidades del lado oeste".</p><p>Para esa misma tormenta de 2023, el Condado de Cook recibió $244 millones del departamento de vivienda federal.</p><p>El dinero de subvenciones de Chicago o del Condado de Cook no se ha gastado.</p><p>¿Está Chicago tomando este problema en serio? Clabe Johnson no lo cree. "Ignoran el problema".</p><p>"Estuve a punto de perder a mi hermano. Nadie explicó la gravedad", dice. "¿Qué pasa con nuestros ancianos? ¿Qué pasa con las personas discapacitadas en esta comunidad que no tienen voz?".</p><p>Fuera de Chicago, Cicero fue golpeado igual de fuerte por la tormenta de 2023 y continúa buscando asistencia gubernamental. Ese mismo año, el poblado de Calumet City experimentó una inundación de 500 años que paralizó la comunidad.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> </div><p></p><h3>El vecindario de Chatham ha estado inundándose durante décadas</h3><p>Mientras estas comunidades han sido golpeadas más recientemente, Chatham ha sufrido desde hace mucho por inundaciones extremas. Una zona baja, Chatham fue construida sobre un antiguo pantano. A mediados y finales de 1800, el área era tan pantanosa que se conocía como “Lago de lodo”.</p><p>Una vez completamente blanca anglosajona, la población de Chatham se volvió predominantemente afroamericana en la década de 1960.</p><p>La familia de Cheryl Watson fue una de los primeros dueños de casa negros en su vecindario, dice. A pesar de décadas de inundaciones en su vecindario, nunca hubo soluciones.</p><p>"Siempre son las comunidades negras porque tenemos la menor representación", dice Watson, quien trabaja en la mitigación de inundaciones. "Todos nos dejan colgando".</p><p>Watson recuerda que su padre bajaba al sótano de su casa familiar en la década de 1970. Junto con otros hombres del vecindario, su papá rutinariamente removía artículos dañados y limpiaba el lodo después de las fuertes lluvias.</p><p>Ahora, con 70 años, Watson regresó a su hogar de la infancia tras la muerte de sus padres. Tiene una bomba de sumidero en el sótano que ayuda con el agua de tormenta, pero eso no ha resuelto completamente el problema.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-bcd350" name="image-bcd350"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>La residente de Chatham, Cheryl Watson, afirma que décadas de inundaciones han causado daños a su hogar en el lado sur.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Chatham tiene varios inconvenientes en su contra. Además de haber sido un pantano, lo que ahora es la Autopista Dan Ryan, construida en la década de 1960, divide la comunidad. Watson dice que se sabe desde hace mucho que Chatham está en un área propensa a las inundaciones de la ciudad. Pero se ha hecho poco para mejorar la situación, asegura.</p><p>"He visto todos estas iniciativas y paradas", dice.</p><h3>Algunas comunidades son golpeadas más que otras</h3><p>Si bien Chatham enfrenta algunos desafíos particulares, se ajusta a un modelo de vulnerabilidad a inundaciones.</p><p>Un análisis de la organización sin fines de lucro Center for Neighborhood Technology, reveló que las comunidades de Chicago con más reclamaciones federales por desastres por inundaciones entre 2007 y 2016 eran casi todas —el 96%— de comunidades de color.</p><p>Incluyen comunidades del lado oeste como Belmont Cragin, Austin y Humboldt Park, y áreas al suroeste hasta Ashburn, luego al este hasta Auburn Gresham y Chatham y al sur hasta Roseland y South Deering.</p><p>"Las comunidades más vulnerables están siendo golpeadas repetidamente", dice Cyatharine Alias, quien es la directora de resiliencia urbana del grupo. "La historia de nuestra región, la falta de inversión en tantas comunidades y la falta de mantenimiento de la infraestructura es lo que está llevando a muchas de las inundaciones".</p><h3>‘Estas personas no tenían un lugar donde dormir’</h3><p>Jacqueline Reed sabe mucho sobre eso. Una vez a la semana, realiza una reunión comunitaria en la iglesia Every Block a Village donde las personas comparten experiencias, trabajan para resolver problemas y rezan.</p><p>La inundación ha sido un tema urgente allí durante los últimos tres años.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Algunas partes del área de Chicago recibieron hasta 9 pulgadas de lluvia durante una tormenta de verano en 2023, lo que inundó los sótanos.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Reed, quien dirige el Westside Long Term Recovery Group, que aboga por ayuda para los sobrevivientes de inundaciones, describe los colchones empapados y objetos valiosos sacados de docenas de apartamentos en sótanos en Austin, muchos de ellos ocupados por ancianos.</p><p>Los voluntarios entraron en aguas hasta la cintura tras las inundaciones de 2023 para ayudar a las personas cuyos hogares se vieron afectados. Colchones y objetos valiosos flotaban en el agua sucia que salía a través de los desagües de la alcantarilla. Grupos de voluntarios ayudaron a limpiar "el lodo".</p><p>"Estas personas no tenían un lugar donde dormir", dice Reed, quien pasó años suplicando a la Municipalidad que proporcionara dinero a las personas afectadas por la inundación.</p><p>Ella y otros ayudaron a recolectar dinero privado y algunas pequeñas cantidades de fondos de la Municipalidad. Pero dicen que es un proceso lento.</p><h3>Cómo hacer que Chicago sea a prueba de inundaciones</h3><p>Las posibles maneras de ayudar a prevenir inundaciones son costosas. Incluyen arreglos que contendrían el agua de inundación a través de tuberías y estructuras tradicionales, así como fuentes naturales, como espacios verdes con plantas y árboles que pueden ayudar a absorber el desbordamiento cuando las alcantarillas están llenas.</p><p>"Uno de los problemas con la gestión de inundaciones en los Estados Unidos hoy es: Ocurre la inundación. Es una emergencia. El gobierno gasta mucho dinero. Construyen exactamente lo mismo que tenían antes", dice Howard Neukrug, profesor de la Universidad de Pennsylvania y antiguo funcionario de la Municipalidad de Filadelfia que lideró la estrategia de gestión de aguas pluviales.</p><p>Chicago y otras ciudades pueden incluir soluciones verdes, pero estas sólo llegarán hasta cierto punto, dice. Se necesitan más túneles y almacenamiento de agua. Y una de las decisiones más difíciles de todas será determinar que algunas áreas esencialmente no son habitables y, en última instancia, tendrán que ser abandonadas.</p><p>Aún así, pequeñas soluciones también pueden hacer una diferencia. Por ejemplo, hay más de 40 parques infantiles en Chicago que están construidos para capturar agua de lluvia debajo en un espacio de contención. Estos parques infantiles incluyen áreas del lado sur y el lado oeste susceptibles a inundaciones.</p><p>Hay un programa de la Municipalidad que ha estado reemplazando lentamente los callejones de superficie dura tradicionales por lo que se conoce como callejones verdes que son permeables y pueden retener agua. El costo de reconstruir un callejón es de aproximadamente $480,000, aunque eso puede variar. Casi 500 han sido instalados desde 2007. Se espera que se instalen más este año, financiados con $20 millones presupuestados para 2026. Chicago tiene casi 2,000 millas de callejones públicos que podrían adaptarse.</p><p>Se necesitan más de estas innovaciones, y deben ser rápidas, según Neukrug y otros.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-369608" name="image-369608"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3ad2525/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F92%2F5e2224684263a14c07239028f318%2Fdelugedbc-floodinghistory-260412-08.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a7e3af8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F92%2F5e2224684263a14c07239028f318%2Fdelugedbc-floodinghistory-260412-08.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/34a5625/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F92%2F5e2224684263a14c07239028f318%2Fdelugedbc-floodinghistory-260412-08.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="El ingeniero civil Richard Fisher se encuentra sobre un lote de tierra propiedad de los Bosques de Conservación del Condado de Cook en Glenwood. Los funcionarios del gobierno están evaluando la viabilidad de convertir el área en humedales para ayudar a controlar las inundaciones." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/34a5625/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F92%2F5e2224684263a14c07239028f318%2Fdelugedbc-floodinghistory-260412-08.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1b08333/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F92%2F5e2224684263a14c07239028f318%2Fdelugedbc-floodinghistory-260412-08.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/34a5625/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2F92%2F5e2224684263a14c07239028f318%2Fdelugedbc-floodinghistory-260412-08.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>El ingeniero civil Richard Fisher se encuentra sobre un lote de tierra propiedad de los Bosques de Conservación del Condado de Cook en Glenwood. Los funcionarios del gobierno están evaluando la viabilidad de convertir el área en humedales para ayudar a controlar las inundaciones.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Richard Fisher, antiguo ingeniero a cargo de proyectos de gestión de aguas pluviales para el Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago (MWRD), lideró muchos de los casi 300 proyectos de control de inundaciones en los que ha trabajado el distrito en poco más de una década. Incluyen proyectos a gran escala, así como los más pequeños de los que habla Neukrug.</p><p>Anteriormente conocido como el Distrito Sanitario de Chicago, el organismo gubernamental que diseñó y construyó el Deep Tunnel y una vez invirtió el flujo del Río Chicago, alejándolo del Lago Míchigan, ahora está buscando soluciones verdes para ayudar con las inundaciones.</p><p>Fisher se encuentra en un campo amplio en Glenwood, justo al oeste del cementerio Assumption Catholic Cemetery and Mausoleum.</p><p>Son 800 acres. Más de una milla cuadrada. Fisher ve un potencial para humedales que serían gestionados por los Reservas Forestales del Condado de Cook.</p><p>Estima que el proyecto ayudaría a reducir las inundaciones para aproximadamente 3,000 hogares, desde Calumet City hasta Dolton y Ford Heights, comunidades que han sido devastadas por inundaciones.</p><p>Fisher lo describe como "realmente gestionando el agua de tormenta donde cae", lo cual es un cambio respecto a la estrategia de desviar el agua de inundación hacia los enormes embalses ubicados en los suburbios.</p><p>El costo podría ser de hasta $300 millones. Aún no está claro si será factible. El tiempo estimado para completar el proyecto podría ser de 10 años o más.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel class="Carousel Enhancement" No hay 800 acres cerca de Chatham, Austin y muchos otros vecindarios de Chicago.</p><p>También hay necesidad de proyectos de ingeniería tradicionales a lo largo de las líneas del proyecto Deep Tunnel.</p><p>Una propuesta para otro enorme proyecto de túneles, para el extremo lado sureste, está siendo considerada por el departamento de gestión de aguas de la Municipalidad. Ese proyecto se conectaría con el Deep Tunnel. El costo se estima en $1,000 millones, para lo cual no se ha encontrado financiamiento.</p><h3>El cambio climático se está intensificando</h3><p>Con la alarmante predicción de que la lluvia en Chicago podría aumentar otra pulgada para 2050, es importante tomar medidas ahora, dicen los expertos. La lluvia no esperará.</p><p>"Estamos viendo cómo el cambio climático realmente mueve esa línea objetivo", dice Kate Evasic, planificadora principal de resiliencia climática para la Chicago Metropolitan Agency for Planning, la principal organización gubernamental de planificación para el área de Chicago.</p><p>Evasic dice que la advertencia que los funcionarios del estado emitieron el año pasado será clave para tomar decisiones.</p><p>"Estamos en un lugar mejor", dice. "Pero tenemos que tener en cuenta la supervisión y los errores que se cometieron".</p><p>Michael Wilson, quien tiene una casa en Auburn Gresham, dice que ha tenido dolores de cabeza por inundaciones durante años. Después de las inundaciones del verano pasado, tuvo agua de alcantarilla que salía por los desagües de su sótano en enero, dejando "un olor a podrido" que "dejaba mucho lodo oscuro y suciedad".</p><p>Hace seis años, experimentó inundaciones en otra propiedad en Chatham. Después de eso, pagó a un contratista más de $12,000 para instalar un sistema de prevención de inundaciones que dice ha funcionado para evitar problemas adicionales.</p><p>"Cuando comienza a llover, sí me da ansiedad", dice Wilson. "Sólo estoy tratando de averiguar el siguiente paso para evitar que este problema vuelva a ocurrir. Espero que la Ciudad de Chicago encuentre una solución".</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" entries.author: Brett Chase

entries.title: Viewership of NBA regular-season games up 86% over last season
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — The numbers are in, and the NBA says Year 1 of its new television deals were a hit.</p><p>The league released numbers for the regular season on Wednesday, showing that 170 million people in the U.S. watched NBA games across the league's four primary broadcast platforms this year — those being ABC/ESPN, Amazon Prime Video, NBC/Peacock, and NBA TV.</p><p>Those numbers are the league's best in 24 years, the NBA said, plus represented an 86% rise over last season.</p><p>Prime Video was part of the league's television rights package for the first time this season and NBC/Peacock returned for the first time in a generation. The league signed a new 11-year, $76 billion-plus media rights deal in 2024 to show games on those two platforms along with ABC/ESPN and NBA TV.</p><p>Those deals kicked in at the start of this season.</p><p>Other highlights of the viewership numbers:</p><p>• NBA games across ABC/ESPN, Amazon Prime Video, NBC/Peacock and NBA TV had the the highest average viewership in 13 years, up 35% over last season.</p><p>• A total of 57 telecasts this season reached an average of 2 million viewers, the most since the 2011-12 season.</p><p>• People watched NBA games for more than 920 million hours, up 25% over last season and the most since 2011-12.</p><p>• The NBA's social media channels generated a record 228 billion views this season, according to Videocites. That's up 13% over last season.</p><p>• Attendance over the past three seasons in NBA arenas is higher than any three-season span in league history.</p><p>• Viewership for NBA Cup group play games was up 90% from last season.</p><p>• The audience for the All-Star Game on NBC, averaging 8.8 million viewers, was the largest for the league's midseason showcase event since 2011.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Treasury says 53 million filers used new Trump tax breaks before the deadline
entries.summary: <p>It's Tax Day on Wednesday — the deadline for most Americans to file taxes — and President Donald Trump's administration says millions of people have already used new breaks such as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/taxes/2026/02/19/no-tax-deductions-trump-irs-filing-season-hype-experts" >no tax on tips</a> and overtime, exemptions for interest on certain car loans, deductions for some seniors, and Trump Accounts for children’s savings.</p><p>More than 53 million filers claimed a deduction under one of those provisions from the administration's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/taxes/2026/02/22/congress-trump-obba-beautiful-bill-federal-irs-tax-returns" >massive tax and spending law</a>, a Treasury official told reporters Tuesday before the deadline, with 6 million people claiming no tax on tips, 21 million claiming the overtime deduction and 30 million <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/taxes/2026/02/20/older-adults-senior-tax-deduction-irs-obbba" >older Americans claiming the enhanced deduction</a>.</p><p>The official, who spoke on condition of anonymity to preview the numbers, said the 2026 filing season was a success from the administration's perspective.</p><p>Still, the latest data comes as most Americans, or 7 in 10, still think their <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/taxes" >taxes</a> are too high, according to recent polling, despite the passage of the Republican tax law which promised big savings for taxpayers.</p><p>Illinois taxpayers who can't meet the April 15 deadline are automatically granted a six-month extension, according to the state's Department of Revenue. Those who file on a calendar-bases would have until Oct. 15.</p><p>But the department said in a news release that an extension doesn't extend the time to pay. "If taxes are owed, taxpayers must pay the amount due by April 15, 2026, to avoid penalties and interest," the department said.</p><p>As the tax season kicked off in January, the White House boasted that average refunds were projected to rise by at least $1,000. But currently, the average refund amount is $3,462, according to the latest IRS data, which is up 11% or about $350 from last tax year’s $3,116 average refund payment.</p><p>“People are getting refunds of $5,000, $8,000, $11,000 that they had no idea they were getting,” Trump said Wednesday on Fox Business News. “It’s turned out to be better — as good or better than I said it would be.”</p><p>The Treasury said tax refunds this season are up 24%, compared with the four-year average of refunds before Trump took office.</p><p>The White House has been trying to promote Trump's tax cuts as a way to get voters more enthusiastic about the way he's handling the economy before November's midterm elections, but the message has been overshadowed for weeks by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/economy/2026/03/25/iran-war-raising-chicago-gas-travel-medicine-food-oil-prices" >higher gas prices</a> caused by the war in Iran.</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p>The 2026 season comes as the IRS has gone through a leadership turnover and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/taxes/2026/02/19/irs-workforce-cuts-obba-tax-laws-delays-filers" >reduced its workforce</a> by 27% over the past year through cuts brought on by the Department of Government Efficiency.</p><p>IRS CEO Frank Bisignano testified to the Senate Finance Committee on Wednesday, extolling the IRS' implementation of the Trump administration's tax law. </p><p>However, Democratic lawmakers have been zeroing in on IRS disclosures of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/taxes/2026/02/26/irs-broke-law-confidential-information-ice-dhs-federal-judge" >confidential taxpayer information</a> to Immigration and Customs Enforcement as part of an agreement between ICE and the Department of Homeland Security to share information for the purpose of identifying and deporting people illegally in the U.S.</p><p><i>Contributing: Sun-Times</i></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/taxes/2026/02/22/trump-accounts-529-savings-tax-changes-obbba-2026" >What taxpayers need to know about savings plans, tax changes in 2026</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/taxes/2026/02/22/congress-trump-obba-beautiful-bill-federal-irs-tax-returns" >Congress passed Trump’s sweeping tax bill. Here’s how it’ll affect your returns</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/taxes/2026/02/26/irs-broke-law-confidential-information-ice-dhs-federal-judge" >The IRS broke the law by disclosing confidential information to ICE 42,695 times, judge says</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: White Sox call up Springfield native Sam Antonacci
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> called up utility player Sam Antonacci on Wednesday, the club's second promotion of a key prospect in as many days. </p><p>Antonacci, a 23-year-old native of Springfield, is rated the No. 9 prospect in the Sox organization and No. 93 overall in MLB by Baseball America. </p><p>After playing for Team Italy in the World Baseball Classic, the Sox' 2024 fifth-round draft pick got off to a strong start at Triple-A Charlotte, where he played left field for the first time in his professional career to pave a quicker path to a Sox roster lacking outfield depth. </p><p>But manager Will Venable had him playing second base and batting fifth in his debut, the second game of the Sox home series against the Rays scheduled for 6:40 p.m.</p><p>Antonacci batted .313/.500/.479 (15-for-48) with two doubles, two home runs, seven RBI and five stolen bases over the first couple weeks of the season. He mostly played second last year and can play across the infield. </p><p>Antonacci's call-up, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/12/white-sox-call-up-noah-schultz-sam-antonacci" >telegraphed over the weekend</a> by team sources, comes a day after the Sox' top pitching prospect, Noah Schultz, made his long-awaited debut. He gave up three runs in the first inning of his first career start, but <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/14/noah-schultz-white-sox-mlb-debut" >rebounded nicely</a>. </p><p>The Sox are expected to welcome a long line of heralded prospects this season, including pitchers Hagen Smith and Tanner McDougal.</p><p>In corresponding moves, the Sox also recalled veteran lefty reliever Tyler Gilbert while optioning Brandon Eisert to Charlotte and designating outfielder Dustin Harris for assignment.</p><p></p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module
