entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Feds in Chicago get first guilty plea in a Midway Blitz case — but not for assaulting immigration agents
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors in Chicago secured their first guilty plea<b> </b>tied to Operation Midway Blitz on Wednesday, though not for the assault charge they’d originally leveled against Anthony Gonzalez Alvarez.</p><p>Instead, he pleaded guilty to concealing<b> </b>information about a crime, admitting that he falsely reported his Ford F-250 stolen to the Chicago Police Department after driving into the rear of a vehicle driven by immigration officers.</p><p>The charge, officially known as “misprision of a felony,”<b> </b>is apparently rare enough that it prompted an in-court discussion of how to pronounce “misprision.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" >Tracking every known federal prosecution in Chicago tied to Trump’s deportation blitz</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>“Never heard of it,” U.S. District Judge LaShonda Hunt<b> </b>told the lawyers.</p><p>Alvarez, 27, is expected to pay $1,362 in restitution<b> </b>but avoid prison under the terms of a deal he struck with prosecutors. However, Hunt must first decide whether to accept or reject the agreement. She’s deferred for now,<b> </b>and she set sentencing for July 22.</p><p>Alvarez is among <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >32 known defendants</a> charged with nonimmigration crimes tied to Midway Blitz, the deportation campaign that rocked the Chicago area last year. Twenty of the 32 have been cleared, and three others are on track to have their cases dismissed.</p><p>Four of the remaining eight are members of the group originally known as the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/16/chicago-broadview-six-kat-abughazaleh-midway-blitz-trump-ice" target="_blank" >“Broadview Six”</a> and face trial May 26. Another man, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/07/new-indictment-cites-shooting-near-border-patrol-agents-in-chicago-still-doesnt-charge-gunman" target="_blank" >Hector Gomez</a>, is set to plead guilty April 27 after being hit with<b> </b>weapons charges tied to Midway Blitz.</p><p>The Trump administration used claims of “harrowing”<b> </b>violence toward federal agents to justify a push to<b> </b>deploy National Guard troops<b> </b>into Chicago last fall. They were blocked initially by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/10/09/federal-judge-set-to-hear-arguments-in-high-stakes-national-guard-hearing" target="_blank" >U.S. District Judge April Perry</a>, and eventually the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/12/23/supreme-court-trump-national-guard-illinois" target="_blank" >U.S. Supreme Court</a>.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/12/23/supreme-court-trump-national-guard-illinois" >U.S. Supreme Court sides against Trump, won’t lift block on National Guard deployment in Illinois</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Even with Alvarez’s guilty plea, no one in Chicago has been convicted in federal court of an act of violence toward agents during Midway Blitz.</p><p>Still, Alvarez admitted that around 10:30 a.m. on Oct. 3, he followed a U.S. Customs and Border Protection vehicle<b> </b>on the South Side. As it turned right from West 61st Street onto South Kedzie Avenue, he drove into the rear passenger side of the feds’ vehicle.</p><p>Later that day, Alvarez told CPD his truck had been stolen, he admitted.</p><p>Alvarez pleaded guilty despite questions about the case previously raised by his defense attorney, Ricardo<b> </b>Meza. The attorney told Hunt in January that<b> </b>“the van that was allegedly hit has been repaired.”</p><p>“It’s not even in the condition that it was in when it was allegedly hit by my client, so we’re not able to inspect it,” Meza told Hunt on Jan. 29. “But despite that, judge, we also want to inspect it because we want to retrieve what’s called the event data recorder.”</p><p>Meza also said he’d be seeking FBI agents'<b> </b>notes because “there’s a lot of confusion about what happened, when it happened, and how many times a vehicle was hit.”</p><p>Assistant U.S. Attorney Stephanie<b> </b>Stern<b> </b>told the judge Meza<b> </b>had the power to subpoena the vehicle himself, from the rental car company that was in possession of the vehicle.</p><p>“It is with Enterprise,” she said during the January<b> </b>hearing.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/16/federal-agent-took-car-from-brighton-park-shooting-back-to-maine-defense-attorney-tells-judge" >Federal agent took car from Brighton Park shooting ‘back to Maine,’ defense attorney tells judge</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Similar issues arose in the case of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/03/marimar-martinez-shot-border-patrol-chicago-speaks-out-after-good-pretti-deaths-i-am-their-voice" target="_blank" >Marimar Martinez</a>, the woman shot<b> </b>by a Border Patrol agent Oct. 4. Shortly after she was accused of driving<b> </b>into<b> </b>that agent’s vehicle, her defense attorney revealed that the agent <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/16/federal-agent-took-car-from-brighton-park-shooting-back-to-maine-defense-attorney-tells-judge" target="_blank" >had taken his vehicle to Maine</a> before the attorney could inspect it.</p><p>Charges were dropped against Martinez in November.</p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: 17 more Chicago cops bilked PPP loan program, including 8 facing firing, inspector general says
entries.summary: <p>Chicago’s top watchdog is heading for the exit — but not before dropping one last fraud bombshell on the Chicago Police Department.</p><p>Inspector General Deborah Witzburg said Wednesday her office has nailed nine Chicago cops in the first four months of the year for allegedly scamming COVID-era relief funds.</p><p>Eight of the officers have been recommended for firing by the police department after investigators dug into their Paycheck Protection Program loan applications — cash meant to keep small businesses afloat during the pandemic in 2020 and 2021. Another cop quit while under investigation.</p><p>All told, the alleged ripoffs totaled $284,000, according to Witzburg’s first quarter 2026 report.</p><p>Eight more of the officers are waiting to find out whether the police department will move to fire them.</p><p>“This was a triage effort, and we are not done yet,” Witzburg said. “The reason we prioritized CPD cases is because they occupy positions of tremendous public trust and they land on the witness stand, so their credibility is of paramount importance.”</p><p>Back in 2023, Witzburg’s office flagged more than 1,000 potential instances of PPP fraud by city employees, including police officers, firefighters and others.</p><p>Her message to anyone who thinks they have slipped through the cracks?</p><p>“You don’t get to defraud the government and work for the government.”</p><p>Witzburg, who steps down at the end of the month, reported late last year that her office found two sergeants and another officer <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/8/18/23836010/ppp-fraud-paycheck-protection-program-covid-relief-chicago-police-department-torrey-price-aaron" target="_blank" >engaged in PPP fraud</a>. The officer had snagged an $18,000 loan for an insurance business that didn’t exist. PPP loans were usually forgiven, meaning they didn’t have to be repaid.</p><p>The police department has agreed to seek the firing of the eight officers Witzburg identified in her latest quarterly report. Her office is waiting for the department to respond to eight other PPP fraud cases that Witzburg sustained. </p><p>She doesn’t name the suspected officers in her reports.</p><p>In a separate federal investigation, former officers Torrey Price and Aaron Price are <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/8/18/23836010/ppp-fraud-paycheck-protection-program-covid-relief-chicago-police-department-torrey-price-aaron" target="_blank" >facing criminal charges</a>. They're accused of raking in more than $2 million by filing about 100 bogus loan applications tied to PPP and another federal aid program.</p><p>The PPP program was rife with fraud amounting to hundreds of billions of dollars. Even people behind bars — and their guards — were <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2024/2/9/24062550/fbi-ppp-pandemic-cook-county-sheriff-tom-dart-jail" target="_blank" >suspected of cashing in</a>, saying they needed help for their phony businesses, the Chicago Sun-Times has found.</p><p>Court records show one Cook County jail guard, Jareli Reyes, was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2024/05/15/ppp-fraud-jareli-reyes-charged-cook-county-correctional-officer" target="_blank" >charged with PPP fraud</a> but acquitted by a Cook County judge at a bench trial last year.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/10/6/23903273/ppp-fraud-gangs-covid-relief-guns-drugs-hit-justice-department-michael-galdo" target="_blank" >Millions in PPP, other COVID-19 relief funds went to gangs, fueled Chicago’s illegal gun market</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2024/2/9/24062550/fbi-ppp-pandemic-cook-county-sheriff-tom-dart-jail" target="_blank" >In twist on PPP, other COVID-19 relief fraud, recordings show Cook County Jail detainees claimed to reap tens of thousands of dollars</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2024/06/07/ppp-fraud-paycheck-protection-program-kickbacks-cha-chicago-housing-authority-covid-relief-sba-illinois-department-human-services-ludeman-center" target="_blank" >PPP fraud was fueled in part by brokers taking kickbacks but escaping punishment, Sun-Times finds</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Frank Main

entries.title: Record-breaking rainfall could bring flash flooding to Chicago area
entries.summary: <p>Many Chicago area residents reported flooding in their homes after record-breaking rainfall on Tuesday.</p><p>O'Hare Airport set a daily record for rainfall Tuesday at 2.43 inches, shattering the previous record set in 1949 when 1.21 inches of rain fell, according the National Weather Service. Tuesday was the rainiest April day at O'Hare since it saw 3.54 inches in 2013. </p><p>The Norwood Park area on the city's Northwest Side accounted for 114 of the more than 600 flooding complaints logged by the city's 311 service request hotline from midnight Tuesday to late Wednesday morning.</p><p>Benjamin Vance, owner of Rescue Plumbing, said his team has been helping clean up 15 homes, with 14 homes still on its waiting list.</p><p>“We're totally booked out,” Vance said. “We can't take any more calls.”</p><p>A <a class="Link" href="https://www.weather.gov/lot/" target="_blank" >flood watch</a> that includes areas around the Des Plaines River in Riverside and River Forest is in effect until Friday evening. </p><p>Meteorologist Mike Bardou said the metro area, all of Northern Illinois and Northwest Indiana are also under a flood watch, and the possibility of tornadoes is minimal.</p><p>“We're concerned about maybe some potentially damaging winds, or kind of marginally severe, like quarter type-size hail,” he said. “The threat is not nearly as great as it was yesterday.”</p><p>“Know your area,” Bardou said. “If you're prone to high water or flooding, or your basement tends to flood if it rains very heavily, be mindful. We're in a stretch where that could very easily happen.”</p><p>Flooding is getting worse in Chicago, according to a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/10/flooding-chicago-climate-change-deep-tunnel-mold-metropolitan-water-reclamation-district-soaked" >Sun-Times/WBEZ investigation</a>. Stronger, climate change-fueled thunderstorms are overwhelming the city's sewers, and rain is falling harder and faster, backing up systems designed to allow water to flow.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/10/flooding-chicago-climate-change-deep-tunnel-mold-metropolitan-water-reclamation-district-soaked" >Flooding in Chicago is getting worse. Here’s why.</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/14/chicago-flooding-mold-health-climate-weather-rain-planning-soaked" >After a flood, mold sticks around and can make people sick</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/consumer-affairs/2026/04/15/what-can-homeowners-do-to-reduce-flooding-climate" >What can homeowners do to reduce flooding?</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: Chicago cab fares going up 20%, as City Council tries to buoy taxi industry
entries.summary: <p>The City Council agreed Wednesday to throw a sorely needed lifeline to Chicago’s shrinking taxicab industry — at the expense of cab riders.</p><p>Without a word of debate, the Council agreed to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/13/chicago-taxicab-fares-increase-20-percent-lyft-uber" >raise taxicab fares</a> by 20%, the first increase in a decade, to help cabdrivers struggling to compete with Uber and Lyft amid skyrocketing gasoline prices and increasing insurance, maintenance and vehicle replacement costs.</p><p>Downtown Ald. Brendan Reilly (42nd) cast the only dissenting vote — even after acknowledging that the taxicab industry has been "eviscerated” by rides-sharing — to protest what he called “rampant abuse" by cabdrivers who fail to pull the flag to start the meter when a passenger enters the cab.</p><p>The fare hike was introduced by Mayor Brandon Johnson last fall while public attention was focused on the battle over his 2026 budget. It has languished in committee ever since — until Wednesday.</p><p>The mayor’s plan would maintain the cost of entering a cab, known as the “flag pull,” at $3.25 for the first one-ninth of a mile, but provide substantial relief for cabdrivers in other ways. That includes a $2.50 rush-hour fee for rides between 3:30 p.m. and 7 p.m., a $1 overnight fee for rides between 8 p.m. and 6 a.m., and increased charges for both time and distance.</p><p>Instead of 25 cents for each additional one-ninth mile, passengers would pay 31 cents. Another 31 cents would be tacked on to the fare for every 45 seconds instead of 20 cents for every 36 seconds.</p><p>Shared rides for “a minimum of two passengers” would also rise — from $24 to $30 from O’Hare Airport to Downtown or McCormick Place — and from $18 to $22 from Midway to Downtown or the convention center. The cost of shared rides between Midway and O’Hare would rise from $37 to $45. The cost of cleaning up a cab after an inebriated or unruly rider would double, from $50 to $100.</p><p>John Moberg, president of Checker, Yellow and American United Cab, said the 20% increase is “long overdue.”</p><p>“Everything has gone up. Our insurance has gone up significantly. The cost of vehicles and the cost to repair vehicles has gone up dramatically. And I don’t have to tell you about gasoline. Everybody is experiencing that,” Moberg said. “ Twenty percent will help cover some of that, but not all of it. We probably need more, but it would be worse for us if we raised it too much because it would chase the riding public away.”</p><p>Chicago’s once-thriving taxicab industry has been shrinking ever since the unregulated advent of ride-hailing services like Lyft and Uber.</p><p>It took years before the city finally stepped in and regulated that industry. By that time, the value of taxicab medallions already had plummeted. A medallion that once sold for as much as $400,000 is now valued at $5,000.</p><p>The pool of available drivers also is shrinking — and aging. Anyone who gets laid off or needs to supplement their income can “go to Uber and be driving tomorrow,” while it takes two or three weeks of classes to be cleared to drive a cab, Moberg said.</p><p>“ The taxi industry is getting older all the time, and people are retiring or dying. We don’t have an infusion of replacements. They want a quick fix. They want money now. They don’t want to wait six weeks to earn money for a cab when they could walk into Uber and be driving the same day,” Moberg said. “If we got that level playing field, we would have a cab industry that could sustain itself into the future, and we could keep that base. We’d be able to get young people. If not, it’ll dry up.”</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Constructor detrás del proyecto de remodelación en McKinley Park recibe una subvención de $250,000
entries.summary: <p>El Grupo IBT fue uno de los dos constructores de Chicago que recientemente recibió una subvención de $250,000 del programa Community First Developer del Banco Federal de Préstamos para Vivienda (FHL) de Chicago, cuyo objetivo es ayudar a los constructores emergentes de bienes raíces y respaldar soluciones de vivienda enfocadas en la comunidad.</p><p>IBT utilizará la financiación para apoyar prácticas profesionales remuneradas y a un investigador principal que ayudará a liderar los esfuerzos de vivienda en el próximo proyecto del constructor: el Terminal Pershing en el vecindario de McKinley Park.</p><p>La inversión de aproximadamente $179 millones transformará el antiguo Quartermaster Depot, ubicado en 1769 W. Pershing Road, en una mezcla de viviendas para trabajadores, laboratorios de ciencia biológica y espacios de oficina, comercio y áreas verdes. Fue construido para el Ejército de los Estados Unidos en 1918.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-250000" name="module-250000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>El proyecto también ayudará a avanzar hacia el objetivo de la Municipalidad de fomentar más desarrollo económico y oportunidades de empleo en McKinley Park.</p><p>“Si recorres esas calles, la mayoría de esas casas están bien cuidadas, son propiedad familiar desde hace mucho tiempo, pero no han visto inversiones significativas”, dijo Gary Pachucki, fundador y presidente de IBT Group. “Esta será una gran inversión, que creará empleos y tendrá un impacto en todo su vecindario. Creo que ayudará a traer vida y nueva vivienda, y luego, con suerte, eso también incentivará más viviendas”.</p><p>IBT fue seleccionado por la Municipalidad para reconstruir el sitio en <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/9/11/23865037/winner-selected-pershing-road-project-reinvent-mckinley-park" target="_blank" >septiembre de 2023</a>, tras las solicitudes de propuestas en 2022 para la rehabilitación del sitio de propiedad municipal. Otras propuestas de remodelación en ese momento incluían un estudio de cine y un espacio comercial de uso mixto.</p><p>El antiguo almacén es un “edificio complicado", dijo Pachucki. El edificio de 571,476 pies cuadrados y seis pisos tiene grandes placas de piso, las cuales Pachucki planea dividir al excavar un patio central.</p><p>“Al quitar el tercio central del edificio, efectivamente creas dos torres de apartamentos, y tienen este gran atrio”, dijo Pachucki. “Esos edificios son realmente sólidos —son todos de concreto— así que será un enorme proyecto de demolición, pero lo que tendrás es este espacio realmente genial”.</p><p>Los planes incluyen alrededor de 172 apartamentos, pero Pachucki dijo que están tratando de que sean 200. Las unidades estarán por debajo del precio del mercado, con el 20% de ellas asequibles, según Pachucki.</p><p>Las unidades, de un promedio de 1,000 pies cuadrados, son también ligeramente más grandes que el apartamento típico debido a la demografía prevista. IBT está dirigiéndose a científicos, trabajadores tecnológicos y otros profesionales de las ciencias que desean vivir cerca de su trabajo. A menudo tienen entre 20 y 30 años y quieren espacio para su pareja o hijos, dijo Pachucki.</p><p>La azotea del edificio incluirá dos espacios de amenidades y áreas verdes.</p><p>La porción al este del sitio de 6.5 acres, que tiene una instalación de mantenimiento municipal de un solo piso, será remodelada para comercio independiente y estacionamiento.</p><p>El Terminal Pershing complementa el proyecto The Terminal de IBT en Humboldt Park. Ese proyecto de $70 millones es el primer campus horizontal de ciencia y tecnología de Humboldt Park, ofreciendo espacios asequibles para empresas emergentes y en crecimiento. Pachucki espera que esa visión se pueda replicar en McKinley Park.</p><p>Pachucki piensa que el Terminal Pershing ayudará a llenar un vacío que enfrentan las empresas emergentes: la falta de espacio de oficina asequible.</p><p>IBT está recaudando fondos para el proyecto, y una vez que esté totalmente financiado, se espera que el Terminal Pershing tarde aproximadamente dos años en completarse.</p><p>Además de IBT, Beauty for Ashes Developers también recibió $250,000. Beauty for Ashes, una firma de propiedad de mujeres y minorías fundada en 2019, utilizará la financiación para expandir sus programas de becas y pasantías.</p><p>Amalgamated Bank of Chicago, miembro del Banco FHL de Chicago, ayudó a los dos constructores a asegurar las subvenciones, que se anunciaron el mes pasado.</p><p>Kimberly Kam, vicepresidenta principal y directora de ventas de servicios de unión y comerciales en Amalgamated Bank de Chicago, dijo que la financiación es clave para los constructores seleccionados. La subvención les permitirá mantener la vivienda asequible al ayudar a cubrir los costos de personal, en lugar de pagar de su propio bolsillo y aumentar los costos del proyecto.</p><p>También amplía las oportunidades para que mujeres y personas de color comiencen en la industria de bienes raíces comerciales, afirmó.</p><p>“La mayor parte de esta subvención es realmente crear y allanar un camino para futuros constructores comerciales”, dijo Kam.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Former charter school executive Tim King charged with stealing more than $100,000 from Urban Prep
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors alleged Wednesday that Tim King – who won national acclaim for founding and serving as CEO of Urban Prep Academies on Chicago’s South Side – stole more than $100,000 from the privately run but publicly funded charter school network.</p><p>In the newly unsealed grand jury indictment, the office of the U.S. attorney in Chicago accused King of taking a total of $103,833.31 by siphoning money from Urban Prep’s bank account to pay his credit card bills.</p><p>King also allegedly tried to obstruct the federal investigation after Urban Prep received subpoenas for records about him in 2022, according to court records.</p><p>King’s defense attorney, Christopher Parente, said King denied wrongdoing and expressed confidence that he would be vindicated in court.</p><p>“Mr. King is innocent, and he will fight these charges aggressively,” Parente said in a statement.</p><p>Parente said the allegations were “fundamentally at odds” with King’s track record of helping Black teens and young men.</p><p>King founded Urban Prep in 2002 and basked in positive press. People Magazine once named King its “Hero of the Year.”</p><p>But WBEZ first reported in June 2022 that Urban Prep <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2022/06/30/celebrated-all-boys-charter-school-in-chicago-cited-by-cps-for-dismal-financial-management" target="_blank" ><u>had been quietly mired</u></a> in such deep financial trouble that Chicago Public Schools officials expressed “grave concerns” about its sustainability.</p><p>The charter network has since been in a legal battle to keep its two schools open within CPS – which has the power to authorize, oversee and fund charter schools across the city.</p><p>Urban Prep once looked at expanding across the country but struggled to make payroll and was operating on credit cards, “predatory loans” and cash advances from the school district, according to a 2022 CPS memo.</p><p>King <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/2022/08/03/star-chicago-charter-school-head-tim-king-forced-out" target="_blank" ><u>resigned as CEO</u></a> in August 2022, after a CPS inspector general’s report alleged that King had inappropriate sexual conduct with a student. King has <a class="Link" href="https://wgntv.com/news/wgn-investigates/wgn-exclusive-ousted-urban-prep-founder-fights-back/" target="_blank" ><u>denied those allegations</u></a> and criticized CPS officials for trying to take action against Urban Prep.</p><p>But by then, court records show, the feds were looking at King. WBEZ reported last year that <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/01/23/feds-sent-subpoenas-to-chicago-charter-school-urban-prep-about-its-former-ceo" target="_blank" ><u>federal prosecutors launched a probe</u></a> into Urban Prep in 2022 and had sought a wide range of documents regarding King.</p><p>When the federal investigation became known publicly, Urban Prep officials defended King, saying, “We do not believe that any current or former Urban Prep employee has engaged in financial improprieties.”</p><p>On Wednesday, King’s successors leading Urban Prep declined to comment on the allegations against him but said in a statement on “today’s unfortunate news” that King’s vision for an all-male charter school “has had a lasting impact.”</p><p>Prosecutors say King diverted more than $54,000 from an Urban Prep bank account to pay charges on his American Express card in 2021, and the following year he allegedly misappropriated more than $49,000 from the school in the same manner.</p><p>Soon after Urban Prep received a subpoena in 2022, authorities say King accessed the software used by the charter operator to track fundraising and deleted some records “with the intent to impede, obstruct and influence the investigation.”</p><p>Court documents show the investigation involved the U.S. Departments of Justice and Education and the IRS.</p><p>Other records obtained by WBEZ show the feds became involved soon after the CPS inspector general’s office expressed deep frustration to the charter operator’s lawyers because Urban Prep officials did not provide requested records. The inspector general’s office had sent Urban Prep a broad request for documents in early 2022.</p><p>But King’s lawyer said Wednesday he had given, not taken from the schools he once led.</p><p>“Over the past 25 years, he spent hundreds of thousands of dollars of his own money to</p><p>build and sustain Urban Prep, including by writing personal checks to ensure the organization met its payroll obligations,” Parente said. “The claim that he would steal from an institution he poured himself into creating is both false and absurd.”</p><p>Under King, Urban Prep boasted that 100% of its mostly-Black male student body had been accepted at colleges.</p><p>Chicago’s school board has tried to revoke Urban Prep’s ability to run schools in CPS and take over its charter schools. But Urban Prep sued CPS and a Cook County Circuit Court judge ruled in the charter network’s favor.</p><p>Two campuses, in Bronzeville and Englewood, remain open for about 200 students. A decade ago, Urban Prep had three campuses with more than 1,300 students.</p><p><i>Dan Mihalopoulos is an investigative reporter for WBEZ. </i></p><p><i>WBEZ education reporter Sarah Karp and Sun-Times federal courts reporter Jon Seidel contributed to this story.</i></p>
entries.author: Dan Mihalopoulos | WBEZ

entries.title: Ex-senior mayoral aide used job to get child hired by city contractor, had firm do unauthorized work, IG says
entries.summary: <p>A Chicago Office of Inspector General report alleges a high-ranking official of a previous mayoral administration used their city title to get a job for their child with a city contractor, then tried to secure nearly $10 million in payments to the contractor.</p><p>A top mayoral aide for Mayor Brandon Johnson stated the onetime official referred to in the report worked for former Mayor Lori Lightfoot’s administration.</p><p>At an unrelated news conference Wednesday, Johnson said the former senior mayoral aide is from a “previous mayoral administration” and that he is doing things differently.</p><p>A spokesperson for Lightfoot said the former mayor “had not been previously made aware of this investigation, and has not received any communication from the OIG.”</p><p>“The allegations in question must be treated with the utmost seriousness,” the spokesperson for Lightfoot said.</p><p>Outgoing Inspector General Deborah Witzburg’s quarterly report, released Wednesday, said the former senior staff member tried to increase the scope of the contractor’s work for the city after that contractor hired their child for a paid internship. The staffer also allowed the contractor to perform unauthorized work for the city, the OIG report found.</p><p>Witzburg's report also stated that the mayoral staffer, whom she did not name, "attempted to facilitate $9.6 million in payments to the contractor to which the contractor was not entitled."</p><p>The $9.6 million in invoices sent from the contractor to the city included false claims and “intentional and negligent billing irregularities,” and they did not comply with the city’s procurement procedures, the OIG report said.</p><p>The former staffer and the president of the contractor discussed the invoices with the city Law Department, the OIG report found, but details of the talks weren’t disclosed. The contractor's identity, and its president, were also not disclosed in the report.</p><p>The OIG report found both the former staff member and the contractor violated several portions of the city’s governmental ethics ordinance, including fiduciary duty, unauthorized use of city property, action on behalf of relatives, and offering, receiving and soliciting gifts or favors.</p><p>Witzburg sent the case to the city’s Board of Ethics, which found probable cause that the former staffer and the contractor each violated the governmental ethics ordinance.</p><p>The city’s Department of Procurement Services has begun preliminary debarment proceedings against the contractor, according to the OIG report.</p><p>Witzburg did not say whether she referred the case to the U.S. attorney’s office.</p><p>“I think situations where people use the authority of a city position to benefit themselves personally — we should all be concerned about those cases,” Witzburg said. “It’s a tremendous privilege to work in the public service, and public servants owe it to the taxpayers to serve the city’s interest first.”</p><p><i>Contributing: City Hall reporter Fran Spielman</i></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: 21 things to do in Chicago April 16-22
entries.summary: <h3>Theater</h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-d05690d0-36e9-11f1-a40d-17f17ad0f282"><li>The Broadway musical, <b>“The Great Gatsby,”</b> based on F. Scott Fitzgerald’s iconic novel, follows the Roaring Twenties story of millionaire Jay Gatsby, who will stop at nothing as he pursues his lost love, Daisy Buchanan. Marc Bruni directs. From April 21-May 3 at Cadillac Palace Theatre, 151 W. Randolph St. Tickets: $49-$140. From April 16-27 at Landmark Century Centre Cinemas, 2828 N. Clark St. Tickets: $12-$17, festival pass $140. entries.author: Mary Houlihan

entries.title: Rahm Emanuel proposes betting and prediction markets tax, defends CPS reforms as he mulls presidential bid
entries.summary: <p>Former Mayor Rahm Emanuel on Wednesday floated a 10% federal tax on online sports betting and prediction markets to boost revenue for science and technology — as he continues to explore a 2028 presidential bid while pressing Democrats to make big changes.</p><p>Emanuel spoke at a Wall Street Journal Live event in Washington about his plan that would help fund artificial intelligence, quantum computing, fusion energy, life sciences and national security technology. The goal would be to beat China in these fields — with an estimated annual revenue of $30 billion to $50 billion flowing into that fund.</p><p>The transaction tax would be placed on the amount wagered or traded on licensed online sportsbooks, digital casino platforms, prediction market apps and exchanges. Emanuel also supports banning all federal employees from prediction markets.</p><p>“We’re stagnant and worse than stagnant under President Trump. We’re cutting the NIH [National Institutes of Health] and the National Science Foundation," Emanuel said. "To me, raise a 10% fee. It goes into this Innovation Fund. I’m tired of people betting against America. I want to bet on America. I want to not reward gambling. I want to actually reward the entrepreneur.”</p><p>One of Emanuel’s key themes — on TV and podcast appearances and op-eds — is that Democrats can’t just focus on resisting President Donald Trump — they need a plan. Gov. JB Pritzker, another presidential hopeful, is echoing the same message. Last week, Pritzker told Rev. Al Sharpton that the next president, the 48th in history, needs an “Agenda 48” to counter Project 2025.</p><p>“We need to have an agenda and we need to make sure we pass it in a first year of a Democratic administration. And it needs to be bold, and it needs to include the restoration of voting rights,” Pritzker said.</p><p>With several 2028 presidential hopefuls in elected office and able to show proof of their governing, Emanuel has been dropping policy plans, including a push for a mandatory retirement age of 75 for the president and across branches of the federal government.</p><p>Emanuel on Wednesday said Democrats need to find “common cause with independents” to build a coalition, and “we better have an agenda for America.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/03/03/rahm-emanuel-democrats-future-economic-club-of-chicago" >Rahm Emanuel says Democrats ‘sunk’ their own party as he mulls his political future</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The former mayor, ambassador to Japan, congressman and White House chief of staff, also voiced support for banning social media apps on phones for those under 16.</p><p>“I don’t want that algorithm raising an adolescent. Australia has done it. France is doing it. Germany’s on the way. Netherlands doing it. Italy’s looking at it. Spain’s already done it," Emanuel said. "And to me, it’s going to be a generational fight, but our kids are going to be better off.”</p><p>As state lawmakers explore a measure that would create environmental, energy and water regulations for data centers, Emanuel said he wants new data centers to be "self-sufficient on energy within the facility itself." </p><p></p><p>Emanuel has blamed Democrats for allowing the country's education system to falter, while instead focusing on race and gender-related issues. He's also concerned about children who fell behind during the pandemic.</p><p>"We did the wrong things during COVID at schools. We don't want to talk about it. We want to look at our shoes and hope nobody notices that we were on the scene of the crime," Emanuel said. "And the fact is, we got to get out of this. ... If we don't shake [it] up and start showing some nerve here, we are going to lose a generation."</p><p>Wall Street Journal Columnist Matthew Continetti brought up Emanuel's well-documented struggles with the Chicago Teachers Union as Emanuel pushed for a longer school day, closed 50 neighborhood schools and dealt with the first teachers union strike in 25 years.</p><p>"I didn't reference it. You did. But OK. I was trying to prevent the PTSD from kicking in," Emanuel said. "I'm about to take double doses when I'm done here." </p><p>Emanuel defended his time as mayor and said he didn't "try to take on the teachers union." He said the academic day and year at Chicago Public Schools was among the country's shortest when he came into office.</p><p> "And I once said to [former CTU President] Karen Lewis, 'What are we fighting about? I don't get this. Both of us want to break the cycle of poverty. Five hours and 15 minutes a day, it's not possible,' — and we also accomplished universal full-day kindergarten, which didn't exist in Chicago," Emanuel said. "As the one person in this room that went toe to toe, they were a challenge. You know what's a bigger challenge? Broken families and poverty. You give me an end to poverty and a family structure that supports a child. Don't worry about the teachers." </p><p>Despite the resistance he faced, Emanuel said he asked three mayors, "'Why don't you take over your public schools?'"</p><p>"And they looked at me and said, 'We watched what happened to you. Why would we want that?'" Emanuel said, adding that he "had to fight" when union members went to his children's schools and held signs that read, "Your dad's an a------" and "Your dad sucks."</p><p>"You have to be willing to do that [take on school reform]," Emanuel said. "And I mean, I'm just going to tell you it's going to take everything in you to do it."</p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: City Council chooses Ald. Villegas as Zoning chair in latest blow to Mayor Johnson
entries.summary: <p>The City Council's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/23/city-council-zoning-committee-chair-vacancy-stalemate-brandon-johnson-jack-lavin" target="_blank" >Zoning Committee chair</a> stalemate that has stalled 85 development projects ended Wednesday with the election of veteran Ald. Gilbert Villegas (36th) to the post and Ald. Derrick Curtis (18th) to replace Villegas as Economic Development Committee chair.</p><p>The reorganization was the latest political defeat for Mayor Brandon Johnson at the hands of an emboldened City Council that rejected the mayor’s corporate head tax during last fall's city budget debate. Villegas and Curtis were part of the renegade City Council majority that approved an alternative budget in defiance of the mayor. </p><p>The City Council took matters into its own hands after Johnson was unable to deliver a majority vote for his two previous favorites to replace now-retired Zoning Committee Chair Walter Burnett: Ald. Byron Sigcho-Lopez (25th) and Daniel La Spata (1st).</p><p>“It certainly is another example of the Council stepping up and sort of rescuing an item to fill a void of what’s not happening on the Fifth Floor [mayor’s office] that’s consistent with what took place with the budget,” said Southwest Side Ald. Marty Quinn (13th), one of Johnson’s most outspoken critics.</p><p>Ald. Brian Hopkins (2nd) said the majority bloc had no choice but to break a stalemate that had largely brought development in Chicago to a halt.</p><p>“It’s the one committee that simply must meet or you get this massive backlog of important legislative matters that would cause real harm to allow them to languish. We can’t let it happen,” Hopkins said.</p><p>In late November, Acting Zoning Committee Chair Bennett Lawson (44th) gave Johnson a<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/11/18/ald-bennett-lawson-acting-zoning-committee-chair-mayor-brandon-johnson-ultimatum" target="_blank" > year-end ultimatum:</a> Make his committee post permanent with control over his own staff or he’s done with the time-consuming job.</p><p>Lawson stuck to his guns by refusing to hold a Zoning Committee meeting in January, though in February he chaired a meeting that included Foundry Park, a scaled-down version of the massive Lincoln Yards development on the North Side.</p><p>He had hoped to convince his colleagues to give him the permanent job when the full City Council met on Feb. 18, but the stalemate continued as some members of the Black Caucus pushed for Villegas to take over the Zoning Committee and Ald. David Moore (17th) to replace Villegas as Economic Development Chair.</p><p>Lawson’s plan to anoint two of his fellow freshman Council members as vice-chairs also alienated veteran alderpersons.</p><p>On Wednesday, Lawson hoped to try again, using a parliamentary maneuver to force an immediate vote on the long-stalled reorganization. But Lawson’s plan unraveled during negotiations aimed at breaking the stalemate.</p><p> Ald. Bill Conway (34th) introduced a revised reorganization brokered by Black Caucus Chair Stephanie Coleman (16th) that anointed Villegas as Zoning chair and Curtis to replace Villegas.</p><p>Villegas thanked his colleagues for their confidence in him and applauded Lawson for holding down the fort twice — once after Zoning Chair Carlos Ramirez-Rosa (35th) <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2023/11/6/23948485/ramirez-rosa-resigns-zoning-chair-city-council-floor-leader-mayor-brandon-johnson" target="_blank" >was forced out</a> over allegations of manhandling an aldermanic colleague, and again after the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/07/17/walter-burnett-son-27th-ward-city-council-seat" target="_blank" >retirement of Burnett.</a></p><p> Villegas vowed to work together with Lawson to “make sure that we’re putting forth policies that help developers build quickly so we can realize the much-needed property tax revenue needed to fund government service.”</p><p>“To those of you that didn’t support me, I want to work with you to figure out how we can continue to move the city forward,” Villegas said. "And you have my commitment that we’ll work together to make sure that projects in everyone’s ward are getting done expeditiously so we can make sure we address affordable housing, market-rate and every other type of building.”</p><p>Johnson took Wednesday's defeat in stride.</p><p>"The city of Chicago is open for business. We're just not for sale," Johnson said. "I'm going to work with anyone who's committed to helping us build the safest, most affordable city in America, whether you call yourself a progressive or whether you call yourself something else. I don't get hung up in those particular ideological titles because I've seen people who have self-proclaimed to be something and they're not."</p><p></p><p></p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Every known federal prosecution in Chicago tied to Trump’s immigration blitz
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors in Chicago have accused 32 known defendants of nonimmigration crimes tied to Operation Midway Blitz. Many were accused of assaulting or resisting federal agents or officers.</p><p>One defendant has pleaded guilty. Twenty have been cleared. Grand jurors refused to indict at least three of the 20, and another member of that group was found not guilty at trial.</p><p>Meanwhile, prosecutors have entered into deferred-prosecution agreements with three additional defendants. In each of those cases, if that person abides by certain terms over a set period of time, the feds have agreed to drop the charges in that case as well.</p><p>The Chicago Sun-Times is tracking all of the known criminal cases tied to Operation Midway Blitz:</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6e0000"
